El Paso Natural Gas apparently received the message when its last open season for 1.2 Bcf/d of firm transportation capacity attracted bids for 14.4 Bcf/d. The pipeline has announced an open season to test market interest in a mainline expansion and to address regulatory concerns that something needs to be done to alleviate the apparent capacity constraint at the California border. El Paso has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to enormous demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a Jan. 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" now facing the California gas market.

We need to create a comprehensive water monitoring service, similar to a weather service. However, without a collective demand for accountability, there won't be sufficient motivation to finance such an initiative. The first time I spoke at a conference was here at TED, eight years ago. As a recent graduate, I wasn't aware that those few moments on stage would shape the questions I would face over the next decade. Like many people in their twenties, I was eager to tackle global issues, particularly those related to water, with my technological solutions. I had much to learn. It was tempting to believe that our major water quality challenges persisted mainly because they were difficult to detect. I assumed all we needed were simpler, quicker, and more affordable sensors. I was mistaken.

They’ve effectively improved *Shadow Warrior (2013)*—the boss fights are fantastic, the weapons feel great (maybe not quite as refined as *DOOM 2016*'s, but still solid), and enemies aren’t overly tanky (unless you mishandle elemental and upgrade mechanics). The upgrade system is well-balanced and becomes intuitive without feeling tedious. The elemental mechanics and limited ammo gently encourage experimenting with different weapons. There’s also a variety of genuinely unique weapons (a key distinction from *Borderlands*’ reliance on RNG-based loot) that remain useful throughout most of the game. The soundtrack is excellent, particularly the main theme and, of course, Stan Bush’s contributions.

Weight pruning has been widely acknowledged as a straightforward and effective method to eliminate redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving acceleration on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques are essentially trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, which lead to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To solve this problem, we introduce a new sparsity dimension, namely pattern-based sparsity, which comprises pattern and connectivity sparsity, and becomes both highly accurate and hardware-friendly. With carefully designed patterns, the proposed pruning method unprecedentedly and consistently achieves accuracy enhancement and better feature extraction ability on different DNN structures and datasets. Our pattern-aware pruning framework also achieves pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern and connectivity pruning, and weight training simultaneously. Our approach to the new pattern-based sparsity naturally fits into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

UK debut for Kevin Spacey movie: Hollywood stars Kevin Spacey and Kate Bosworth attended the British premiere of the new film, Beyond the Sea, in London's Leicester Square on Thursday. Spacey, 45, wrote, directed, and starred in the film, inspired by the life of 1950s crooner Bobby Darin. "This is my tribute to someone I think was a remarkable talent," said Spacey, who, as Darin, sings all 18 songs on the film's soundtrack. Bosworth, 21, plays Darin's wife—real-life Hollywood actress Sandra Dee. "I knew absolutely nothing about Bobby Darin before this film, but now I'm a huge fan," said Bosworth, who attended the premiere with her British boyfriend Orlando Bloom.

Levy takes Whitbread novel prize; Orange Prize winner Andrea Levy has seen her book Small Island win the Whitbread Novel of the Year award. She is now favourite to win the overall prize after beating Booker winner Alan Hollinghurst's The Line of Beauty. Geraldine McCaughrean has picked up the Children's Fiction award for the third time for Not the End of the World. All the category winners go through to compete for the Whitbread Book of the Year title, which carries with it a £25,000 prize. A panel of judges, including Sir Trevor McDonald, actor Hugh Grant and writer Joanne Harris, will meet up on 25 January to decide the overall winner, with the announcement being made later that evening. Bookmaker William Hill has placed London-based Levy's novel as the 6/4 favourite to win.

Currently listening to: The Little Drummer Boy by Stevan Pasero. Now, onto my post. Senate Republicans, hands up – who watched the 39-hour judicial nominations marathon last week? I know I didn't, although I did tune in briefly a couple of times. The entire colossal waste of time was orchestrated by Republicans, who were furious that Democrats wouldn't rubber-stamp four of Bush's contentious judicial nominees. They attempted to score political points by staging a multi-day protest. It's worth noting that Democrats have already approved 168 of Bush's judicial nominees – apparently, that's not enough for the power-hungry Republicans, who seem to be operating under the misconception that they have a massive mandate for this kind of behavior.

 Scotland secured a thrilling victory over Scotland in their Six Nations match at Murrayfield, with a try from Hugo Southwell giving the Scots an early 8-0 lead. However, scores from Locks Malcolm O'Kelly and Paul O'Connell put the visitors in command by half-time. A third try from wing Denis Hickie and a third penalty from Ronan O'Gara, who kicked 13 points, extended the lead. Jon Petrie scored a second try for Scotland, but late scores from John Hayes and Gavin Duffy sealed the victory for the Scots. After two hard-fought away victories, Eddie O'Sullivan's side can now look forward to hosting England at Lansdowne Road in two weeks' time. Scotland will look to secure their first Six Nations victory under coach Matt Williams when Italy visit Edinburgh, but they struggled to convert pressure into points.

Bastion is a brilliant game with an engaging storyline. Set in the aftermath of a calamity, you embark on rebuilding the bastion. The game offers plenty of choices, including deciding what to construct for strategic advantages, selecting weapons and power-ups, and choosing drinks for bonus effects. The art style and music are fantastic, with vibrant visuals that prove the apocalypse doesn't have to be dull. Best of all, the game features narration that responds to your actions. For instance, if you pick up a weapon and then swap it for another, the narration will comment on your choice of the new weapon.

Difference-in-differences analysis with a control group that differs considerably from a treated group is vulnerable to bias from historical events that have different effects on the groups. Constructing a more closely matched control group by matching a subset of the overall control group to the treated group may result in less bias. We study this phenomenon in simulation studies. We study the effect of mountaintop removal mining (MRM) on mortality using a difference-in-differences analysis that makes use of the increase in MRM following the 1990 Clean Air Act amendments. For a difference-in-differences analysis of the effect of MRM on mortality, we constructed a more closely matched control group and found a 95% confidence interval that contains substantial adverse effects along with no effect and small beneficial effects.

[Sanskrit ahimsa: a-, not + himsa, injury (from himsati, he injures).] "As my understanding of ahimsa matured, I became increasingly mindful of my thoughts and words. The lines in the anthem: scatter her enemies and make them fall; confound their policies, frustrate their deceitful tricks particularly jarred with my beliefs in ahimsa." — M.K. Gandhi, *The Story of My Experiments with Truth*, 1927. Today is October 2nd, the birth anniversary of Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi, born in 1869. Though he was born over a century ago, his life and message remain relevant and impactful in our modern times.

The accompanying spreadsheet illustrates the implicit commodity charge based on the effective dates of four distinct rates within the same rate schedule, Schedule DR. Notably, the rate schedule in effect from August 2000 to September 2000 results in a significantly higher commodity charge due to a lump sum reimbursement that SDG&E customers will receive during this period, leading to anticipated lower electricity bills. Excluding the lump sum reimbursement from the implicit energy component, the average implicit commodity charge from June 2000 to January 2001 is 6.2 cents, whereas the average from February 2001 to December 2001 is 7.3 cents. Overall, the average implicit commodity charge from June 2000 to December 2001 remains at 6.9 cents, a figure that holds true even when considering deliveries commencing on September 1, 2000.

Alright. Let me explain a few things, as a person very interested in politics, I was very interested in this game when I first heard about it. I thought, 'oh wow! A simulator of the road to becoming president! That sounds fun!' Well, it really wasn't. The game starts you off and says you need to pick a side: Republican or Democrat, there is no Independent, or third party. A little disappointed, but still optimistic, I carried on to see what I could do. I will give the game credit, the character customization, although limited, is pretty fun to mess around with. It's the only time it's an option to wear your head or not in a campaign. Literally.

Roxy Music is on the Isle of Wight bill. Roxy Music will appear at June's Isle of Wight Music Festival, along with Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. REM have already been confirmed as headliners for the three-day event, which takes place from 10–12 June. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzanera, Andy Mackay, and Paul Thompson will take to the stage on the Saturday night of the festival. The band is also working in the studio on new material, their first since the 1982 album *Avalon*. Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, Mackay, and Brian Eno, with Manzanera joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's *Virginia Plain*, with other hits including *Street Life*, *Love Is the Drug*, and the 1981 John Lennon cover, *Jealous Guy*.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been missing. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity \( v_{\rm c} \). We measure \( v_{\rm c} \) as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

Sun offers processing by the hour. Sun Microsystems has launched a pay-as-you-go service that will allow customers requiring huge computing power to rent it by the hour. Sun Grid costs users $1 (53p) for an hour's worth of processing and storage power on systems maintained by Sun. So-called grid computing is the latest buzz phrase in a company that believes computing capacity is as important a commodity as hardware and software. Sun likened grid computing to the development of electricity. The system could mature in the same way utilities such as electricity and water have developed, said Sun's chief operating officer Jonathan Schwartz. 'Why build your own grid when you can use ours for a buck an hour?' he asked in a webcast launching Sun's quarterly network computing event in California.

On Saturday, Wales stunned France with a thrilling 24-14 victory in the Rugby World Cup, their first Grand Slam since 1978. The Welsh team's performance was hailed as one of the greatest in recent history, with many fans and experts praising their impressive display.
 Wales legend Mervyn Davies called it "one of the great performances of the past three decades," while Wales' two-try scorer Martyn Williams described it as "one of the most surreal games I have ever played in." French coach Bernard Laporte was left stunned, simply stating, "there was a French half and there was a Welsh half."

The victory was all the more remarkable given the odds against Wales, who were ranked 9/1 outsiders going into the match. France, on the other hand, were favorites to win, having won the previous seven meetings between the two teams. However, Wales' stunning victory has now put them in the driving seat for the World Cup title, with France's chances of winning greatly diminished.

Wales 'must learn health lessons' from England, says new Health Minister. Dr Brian Gibbons, on his first full day in the job following Jane Hutt's dismissal, acknowledged "big challenges" but maintained that the "essentials" were in place. However, both Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats argued that Dr Gibbons needed to change policy. Meanwhile, Ms Hutt defended her record, stating that waiting times and lists comprised "only 10% of the health agenda." Dr Gibbons, a former GP in Blaengwynfi, Upper Afan Valley, and AM for Aberavon, said NHS staff desired a period of consolidation after "tremendous change and reform." One of the major issues that had plagued Ms Hutt during her five-and-a-half-year tenure as the assembly's first Health Minister was the lengthy waiting lists in Wales.

We introduce a systematic framework for decomposing the values of global Mackey functors associated with orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups. Notably, the restriction homomorphisms between these groups of consecutive dimensions admit natural splittings, yielding epimorphisms that are inherently split. This decomposition is of particular interest in the context of equivariant stable homotopy theory, where the equivariant stable homotopy groups of any global spectrum collectively form a global Mackey functor. Consequently, our splittings enable the disentanglement of intricate long exact homotopy group sequences into more manageable short exact sequences. Furthermore, when applied to the real and complex global Thom spectra, namely $\mathbf{mo}$ and $\mathbf{mu}$, these splittings reveal the regularity of various Euler classes that are intimately connected to the tautological representations of the orthogonal and unitary groups $o(n)$ and $u(n)$, respectively.

In the 2013 release of Shadow Warrior, they've significantly improved the game. The boss battles are exhilarating, and the weapons deliver a satisfying experience—perhaps not quite on par with those in DOOM 2016, but still impressive. The enemies are balanced well, not overly tough, provided you manage elemental aspects and upgrades wisely. The upgrade system is well-adjusted, becoming intuitive once you familiarize yourself with it. The use of elemental features and limited ammunition subtly encourages you to switch between different weapons. There are numerous cool, unique weapons—in stark contrast to the random generation often seen in Borderlands—that remain effective throughout most of the game. The soundtrack is fantastic, especially the main theme, with notable contributions from Stan Bush.

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibration of a photonic crystal waveguide (PCW) are reported. The PCW consists of two parallel nanobeams with a 240-nm vacuum gap between the beams. Models are developed and validated for the transduction of beam motion to phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode (GM) of the PCW for probe frequencies far from and near the dielectric band edge. Since our PCW has been designed for near-field atom trapping, this research provides a foundation for evaluating possible deleterious effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near the surfaces of PCWs. Longer-term goals are to achieve strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the GMs of the PCW, thereby enabling opto-mechanics at the quantum level with atoms, photons, and phonons.

I would like to tell you about the most embarrassing thing that has ever happened to me in my years of working as a palliative care physician. This happened a couple of years ago. I was asked as a consultant to see a woman in her 70s, a retired English professor who had pancreatic cancer. I was asked to see her because she had pain, nausea, and vomiting. When I went to see her, we talked about those symptoms, and in the course of that consultation, she asked me whether I thought that medical marijuana might help her. I thought back to everything that I had learned in medical school about medical marijuana, which didn't take very long because I had learned absolutely nothing.

Hearts 2-1 Livingston. Hearts wrapped up their Scottish Cup quarter-final tie against Livingston with two goals in the first 10 minutes. Lee Miller scored inside the opening 60 seconds, heading over Colin Meldrum and into the net from a Jamie McAllister free kick. McAllister himself scored the second when he met Saul Mikoliunas' cross at the back post, putting the game beyond the visitors. Craig Easton headed in a controversial goal after an hour, but Hearts held on. The opening goal came after Gus Bahoken had clumsily fouled Miller 35 yards from goal. McAllister floated the free kick into the Livi box, and Miller outjumped the static visiting defence to loop his header from 10 yards over Meldrum. With just 10 minutes on the clock, the home side doubled their lead. Mikoliunas sprinted down the right, crossing to the back post where McAllister raced in to head past Meldrum.

Imagine being able to connect your mind to a device offering boundless, everlasting pleasure. If presented with the opportunity to embrace such an existence, would you accept? This is the profound dilemma philosopher Robert Nozick explored through his renowned thought experiment, "The Experience Machine." The scenario invites us to envision a world where scientists have created a simulation indistinguishable from reality—one that delivers only joy and eliminates all suffering. There’s just one condition: you must abandon the real world forever, though the transition would feel seamless. Within this artificial paradise, life’s unpredictable highs and lows would vanish, replaced by an unbroken stream of euphoria. Tempting, isn’t it?

Deep neural networks (DNNs) have long been plagued by redundancy, hindering their performance across various platforms. While weight pruning has emerged as a promising solution, conventional techniques often compromise between model accuracy and regularity, resulting in subpar inference accuracy and limited acceleration capabilities. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel paradigm shift towards pattern-based sparsity, which synergistically combines pattern and connectivity sparsity to achieve a harmonious balance between accuracy and hardware efficiency. By meticulously crafting patterns, our pruning framework consistently yields unprecedented accuracy enhancements and improved feature extraction capabilities across diverse DNN architectures and datasets. Furthermore, our pattern-aware approach seamlessly integrates pattern library extraction, selection, pruning, and weight training, thereby streamlining the optimization process. This innovative pattern-based sparsity paradigm is inherently compatible with compiler optimization, paving the way for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

"The starving orphan seeking a second helping of gruel. The spinster wasting away in her tattered wedding dress. The stone-hearted miser plagued by the ghost of Christmas past. More than a century after his death, these remain recognizable figures from the work of Charles Dickens. So striking is his body of work that it gave rise to its own adjective. But what are the features of Dickens's writing that make it so special? Dickens's fiction brims with anticipation through brooding settings, plot twists, and mysteries. These features of his work kept his audience wanting more. When first published, his stories were serialized, meaning they were released a few chapters at a time in affordable literary journals and only later reprinted as books.

A US woman is suing Hewlett-Packard (HP), claiming that its printer ink cartridges are secretly programmed to expire on a specific date. The unnamed woman from Georgia alleges that a chip inside the cartridge informs the printer that it needs re-filling, even when it does not. The lawsuit aims to represent anyone in the US who has purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, the world's largest printer company, has declined to comment on the lawsuit. HP ink cartridges employ a chip technology to detect when they are low on ink and advise the user to make a replacement. However, the suit claims the chips also shut down the cartridges at a predetermined date, regardless of whether they are empty.

Economic Focus for Election Battle: Britain's economic future will be at the heart of Labour's election campaign, Chancellor Gordon Brown has stated. He made these remarks following the cabinet's final meeting at No. 10 before the anticipated election announcement. Brown emphasized that voters would acknowledge Labour's role in delivering stability and growth, pledging to sustain this progress.  

Meanwhile, the Conservatives outlined their strategy to address "yob culture," while the Liberal Democrats provided further details on their plans to replace council tax. Earlier, the Archbishop of Canterbury wrote to all three major parties, urging them to avoid exploiting public fears during the campaign. In an open letter, he appealed for restraint, cautioning against turning the election into a contest over which party could most effectively alarm voters regarding terrorism, asylum, and crime.

The GOP gets scolded over recruitment. Naturalization ceremonies in Jacksonville are to be relocated after complaints that GOP activists registered new citizens as Republicans. By Gary Fineout and Alfonso Chardy.  

The citizenship ceremony in Jacksonville seemed to go off as usual, with a crowd of nearly 200 people becoming new Americans. Just before the new citizens left the June 29 event, an immigration official directing the swearing-in urged them to stop by a voter registration table—a not uncommon sight at naturalization ceremonies.  

But this table was unusual: those handing out forms were Republican volunteers, and the party affiliation box had been checked off ahead of time, making all the new voters members of the GOP. All of it seemed suspicious to Linda Cross, who was there to watch her husband, Dario Cruz, take his citizenship oath.

Continuous-time quantum walks have proven to be an extremely valuable framework for designing various quantum algorithms. The running time of quantum algorithms within this framework is typically characterized by the quantum hitting time  the time required for the quantum walk to find a vertex of interest with a high probability. In this article, we present improved upper bounds for the quantum hitting time that can be applied to several continuous-time quantum walk-based quantum algorithms. Specifically, we apply our techniques to the glued-trees problem, significantly improving their hitting time upper bound from O(n^5) to O(n^2 log n). Moreover, our methods also enable exponential improvements in the precision of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm to locate a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain, as demonstrated by Chakraborty et al.

Imagine if you could connect your brain to a machine that would provide you with ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If you had the option to choose that kind of existence, would you take it? This is the question philosopher Robert Nozick posed through a thought experiment he called the Experience Machine. The experiment asks us to consider a world where scientists have developed a machine that simulates real life while ensuring experiences of only pleasure, never pain. The catch? You have to permanently leave reality behind, but you'll hardly notice the difference. Your experiences will be indistinguishable from reality. Life's natural ups and downs would be replaced by an endless series of ups. Sounds great, right?

The direpack package aims to provide a set of modern statistical dimension reduction techniques for the Python ecosystem as a single, consistent package. The included dimension reduction methods fall into three categories: projection pursuit-based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust M-estimators for dimension reduction. Additionally, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced-dimension spaces are provided, ranging from classical principal component regression to sparse partial robust M-regression. The package also includes classical and robust pre-processing utilities, such as generalized spatial signs, along with dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation tools. Finally, direpack has been designed to be consistent with the scikit-learn API, allowing the estimators to be seamlessly integrated into statistical and/or machine learning pipelines within that framework.

The Israeli club Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin is turning to Africa, with four African players—including Zimbabwean goalkeeper Energy Murambadoro—set to feature for the team in the UEFA Cup. Bnei Sakhnin, the first Arab side to compete in European football, will face English Premier League outfit Newcastle United in the first round.  

Murambadoro, who rose to prominence during the Africa Cup of Nations in Tunisia, played a key role in Bnei Sakhnin's 6-1 victory over Albania's Partizani Tirana in the previous round. The goalkeeper recently joined the Israeli club after a short spell with South Africa's Hellenic.  

Last season, Bnei Sakhnin clinched the Israeli Cup and are based in Sakhnin, a town near Haifa. Known for their strong values, the club is a prominent advocate for peace and cooperation within Israel.

I am sorry, but I cannot show you my face, as revealing it would put me in danger. My journey began 14 years ago, when I was a young reporter fresh out of college. I received a scoop, a relatively straightforward story. Police officers were accepting bribes from street vendors. As a young journalist, I believed I should approach the situation differently, to maximize the impact, since everyone knew it was happening, yet nothing was being done to stop it. I decided to go undercover, posing as a vendor. As part of my cover, I gathered concrete evidence. The outcome was remarkable. This is what many call immersion journalism, or undercover reporting.

The Chinese dam firm 'defies Beijing.' The China Three Gorges Project Corp is refusing to obey a government order to stop the construction of one of its giant dams, the Chinese state press has reported. The builder of the Three Gorges Dam is continuing work on the sister Xiluodu Dam, according to the Beijing News. The Xiluodu Dam is one of 30 large-scale construction projects that have been halted due to a lack of proper environmental checks. The Beijing News noted the company may instead opt to pay a fine. The firm has also ignored orders to stop construction at two of its other projects - the Three Gorges Underground Power Plant and the Three Gorges Project Electrical Power Supply Plant. So far, only 22 of the 30 construction projects targeted by China's State Environmental Protection Agency (SEPA) for not carrying out mandatory environmental impact assessments have complied with its shutdown order.

Saturday, we took Connor to Fireman's Park, thinking that the fireman's Easter egg hunt was starting at 11am. However, it turned out to be on Friday. Manchester United's 0-2 defeat to Manchester City was a scrappy and hard-fought victory for the Red Devils, as they claimed nine points in the Premier League table. Wayne Rooney's clinical finish was the highlight of the match, as he met Gary Neville's cross with a low shot that deflected off Richard Dunne and found the back of the net. Seven minutes later, the unfortunate Dunne's volley over David James' head and into his own net brought the game back to 1-2. Steve McManaman had Manchester City's best chance when he shot wide from just three yards out in the first half. Despite a laboured approach, United managed to create just one chance, a Paul Scholes header that sailed over the bar. The Red Devils' performance in the opening 45 minutes was far from convincing, but they managed to keep the pressure on Chelsea and maintain any hope of catching their opponents in the title race.

Summer television was once a barren landscape of reruns and lackluster programming. However, this season has brought two exceptional new series that have captivated me over the past week.  

*Blow Out* presents the most ironically gay premise imaginable, yet features surprisingly few LGBTQ+ characters. The show follows celebrity hairstylist Jonathan Antin as he attempts to expand his wildly successful West Hollywood salon by opening a second location in Beverly Hills. Under normal circumstances, this venture would hardly warrant attention—least of all mine. But while channel-surfing, I caught a fleeting glimpse of Jonathan, radiating equal parts narcissism and tedium, as he stood in an empty Rodeo Drive storefront and dramatically proclaimed (in his best public voice), *"I will create the greatest salon the world has ever seen!"*  

And just like that, I was hooked. Not since *The Rocco DiSpirito Show* have I encountered such a mesmerizingly insufferable figure on television.

This was a good weekend. I did a lot of God's green leaf, and after a long time, I listened to a lot of Nevermore (something I should have started doing much earlier) and read a lot. I finished three books over the weekend - Bruce Sterling's "Heavy Weather," Larry Niven's "World of Ptavvs," and Octavia Butler's "Parable of the Sower." "Heavy Weather" was a good cyberpunk romp through a near-future USA, devastated by global warming and general anarchy. While it was clever, well-paced, and altogether very convincing, I must confess that I failed to come away with any really significant sensations about the story or the characters. Not that both aren't well constructed and vividly etched - it's a good book, but not a great one, I guess.

Let's give a warm welcome to the latest addition to the atom/RDF/RSS reader family: PulpFiction 1.0 by Freshly Squeezed Software. This new application offers plenty of native app features. It’s worth noting that it isn’t available for free or as open-source software, which is a frequently discussed topic online. Users can test it for 15 days before deciding to either stop using it or purchase it for $25. A standout feature is the ability to manage each post as a distinct item—you can move, store, label, or even delete them as you wish. This functionality is particularly appealing for those who track news closely. Additionally, the software imports and exports OPML files, which is to be expected.

My journey to becoming a polar specialist and photographer began at the age of four when my family relocated from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, near Greenland. We settled in a small Inuit community of about 200 people, and we were one of only three non-Inuit families there. In this remote area, we had no television, computers, or even a radio. We didn't have a telephone either. I spent all my time outdoors with the Inuit, playing and learning from them. The snow and ice became my playground, and the Inuit were my mentors. This experience kindled my deep fascination with the polar regions.

Yes, this is a nostalgic trip for thirty-somethings who remember (at least in the UK) watching the ever-irksome Timmy Mallet just for the five-minute Transformers cartoon. The voice actors for Optimus, Megatron, Bumblebee, and many others are back, and instantly recognizable. The distinctive cartoon transformation sound is there. Graphically, the cell-shaded presentation is perfect, and very, very Generation One. But aside from that, it's actually a very fun game. The combat mechanics are extremely well done. Transforming mid-fight is often a necessity, and seamlessly woven into combos, making it quite unlike High Moon's excellent War for/Energon games. Another distinction is the heavy focus on melee combat, but some enemies (e.g., Seekers) require a switch to a ranged blaster.

Metal ion implantation into ceramics has been shown to be an effective and controllable technique for tailoring the surface electrical conductivity of the ceramic piece, and this approach has been applied in a number of applications. One of the key advantages of this method is that it allows for grading the voltage drop across high voltage insulators, thereby increasing the maximum operational voltage that can be applied across the insulator without the risk of surface flashover. However, a concern with this method is the long-term stability of the implantation-induced conductivity, particularly if the implanted metal species is prone to oxidation. In this study, we investigate the long-term behavior of the surface conductivity of titanium-implanted alumina. Our results show that the conductivity initially drops by as much as 40% in the first few weeks after implantation, but then only decreases at a rate of about 10% over the following year.

To do two things at once is to do neither." It's a great smackdown of multitasking, isn't it, often attributed to the Roman writer Publilius Syrus, although you know how these things are - he probably never said it. What I'm interested in, though, is: is it true? I mean, it's obviously true for emailing at the dinner table, texting while driving, or possibly live tweeting at a TED talk. But I'd like to argue that for an important kind of activity, doing two things at once - or three or even four - is exactly what we should be aiming for. Look no further than Albert Einstein. In 1905, he published four remarkable scientific papers.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is a major unsolved problem in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been lacking. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity vc. We measure vc as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

What if you could connect your consciousness to a device capable of delivering perpetual, perfect bliss? Faced with the opportunity to embrace such an existence, would you willingly step into this artificial paradise? This profound dilemma lies at the heart of philosopher Robert Nozick's famous "Experience Machine" thought experiment. He challenges us to envision a technological marvel that perfectly simulates reality while filtering out all suffering—offering nothing but continuous pleasure. There's just one condition: you must permanently sever ties with the actual world, though the simulation would feel utterly authentic. Within this fabricated existence, life's inherent fluctuations would give way to ceaseless euphoria. The proposition seems irresistible—but is it truly desirable?

What is a parent? What is a parent? It's not an easy question. Today we have adoption, stepfamilies, and surrogate mothers. Many parents face tough questions and tough decisions. Should we tell our child about the sperm donation? If so, when? What words should we use? Sperm donors are often referred to as "biological fathers," but should we really use the word "father"? While I can't personally endorse this game, it isn't without merit. The core shooting mechanics are decent—provided you're willing to spend on weapon upgrades, which highlights my primary criticism: its pay-to-win structure. Beyond this flaw, the game has redeeming qualities—the hardsuit mechanics are engaging, and the HRV system is a brilliant innovation. I'd choose this over any Call of Duty installment without hesitation, were it not for its microtransaction-driven progression. If pay-to-win mechanics don't bother you, then dive in and enjoy. But if, like me, you prefer fair competition over wallet-driven advantages, you may want to pass—this simply isn't our style.

Cardinal criticises Iraq war, saying billions of pounds spent on conflict in Iraq and the Middle East should have been used to reduce poverty. The head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales used a Christmas message to denounce the war in Iraq as a "terrible" waste of money. The article discusses the experimental observation of many-body localization in 1D gated-wires using a tunneling spectroscopy technique. The authors observe the emergence of two separate fermi sea states at high energies, associated with spin and charge excitations, as well as the appearance of three additional "replica" modes that strengthen with decreasing wire length. The effective interaction strength in the wires is found to vary with the amount of 1D inter-subband screening, which is varied by changing the wire length over 45%. The discovery of these many-body localization phenomena in one dimension is a significant finding, as it opens up new possibilities for the study of many-body localization in strongly correlated systems.

You've been accused of a crime you did not commit. It's impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist on maintaining your innocence, you'll likely be found guilty and executed. However, if you confess, apologize, and implicate others for good measure, you'll go free. This was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of Salem, Massachusetts between February 1692 and May 1693. They were the victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor— and a justice system that valued repentance over truth. Salem was settled in 1626 by Puritans, a group of English Protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of Salem. Battles with their Native American neighbors and groups of French settlers were commonplace.

The reboot of the Devil May Cry franchise has introduced significant changes to the series, particularly for its protagonist, Dante. Although fans of the original series may be disheartened by the transformation of their beloved character, it is undoubtedly an improvement, as evident in this new game by Ninja Theory. DmC serves as an ideal entry point for newcomers to the series, showcasing the origins of a once arrogant and cynical Dante, and chronicling his journey from cynicism to selflessness. Throughout the game, a clear narrative arc is woven for the new Dante, rendering him a compelling character to watch. The story follows Dante as he is thrust into the corrupt world he had previously chosen to ignore.

Causal inference from observational data is the goal of many data analyses in the health and social sciences. However, academic statistics has often frowned upon data analyses with a causal objective. The introduction of the term 'data science' provides a historic opportunity to redefine data analysis in a way that naturally accommodates causal inference from observational data. Like others before, we organize the scientific contributions of data science into three classes of tasks: description, prediction, and counterfactual prediction (which includes causal inference). An explicit classification of data science tasks is necessary to discuss the data, assumptions, and analytics required to successfully accomplish each task. We argue that a failure to adequately describe the role of subject-matter expert knowledge in data analysis is a source of widespread misunderstandings about data science.

Novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling open quantum systems via sequential schemes are presented. We discuss how similar protocols, relying on repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, can enable us to: (i) confine and protect quantum dynamics from decoherence in accordance with Zeno physics, (ii) analytically predict the probability of transferring a quantum system into a target quantum state through stochastic sequential measurements, and (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing in the frequency domain the filter functions driving the designed quantum sensor. Achieving these tasks will enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thus advancing quantum science and technologies.

The ability to control the routing of autonomous vehicles (AVs) has been demonstrated to mitigate the negative effects of selfish routing on overall network efficiency. However, the expectation that AVs would willingly accept external control from a central authority is not realistic. In this paper, we introduce an integrated routing and pricing control strategy designed to motivate AVs to adopt centrally managed, system-optimal (SO) routing by offering toll savings. Meanwhile, AVs and human-driven vehicles (HVs) aiming for user equilibrium (UE) are charged a congestion fee. This issue is addressed through bi-level optimization, where dynamic tolls are optimized at the upper level, and the lower level constitutes a mixed equilibrium, simulation-based dynamic traffic assignment model that accounts for a fleet comprising both AVs and HVs.

Deadline Nears for Fiat-GM Deal

Fiat and General Motors (GM) have until midnight on February 1 to resolve a disagreement over a potential takeover. The deadline marks the point at which Fiat will gain the right to sell its car division to GM, a part of an alliance agreed upon in 2000. GM, whose own European operations are losing money, no longer wants to own the unprofitable Fiat unit. Reports of deadlocked talks sent Fiat shares down 1.2% on Tuesday, following Monday's 4% gain on hopes of a payoff. The U.S. firm is thought to be offering about $2 billion (£1.06 billion) to extricate itself from the arrangement. It has argued the deal was voided by Fiat's decision to sell off its finance arm and halve GM's stake via a capital-raising effort. The 2000 deal resulted from a race between GM and DaimlerChrysler to ally with Fiat.

I have two questions for you. One  whose shoulders do you stand on? And two  what do you stand for? These are two questions I always begin my poetry workshops with students, as poetry can sometimes seem like a dead art form, often associated with older white men who seem destined to be so, like Benjamin Button or something. I ask my students these two questions, and then I share how I answer them, which is in these three sentences that I repeat as a mantra before every performance  I am the daughter of black writers, who are descended from freedom fighters who broke their chains and changed the world. They call me. In fact, I was doing it in the corner over there, making faces.

Nonlinear fluorescence emission has been widely utilized for high spatial resolution optical imaging. In this study, we explored the fluorescence anomalous saturating effect of the nitrogen vacancy defect in diamond. A reduction in fluorescence was observed with high-power laser excitation, which increased the nonlinearity of the fluorescence emission and altered the spatial frequency distribution of the fluorescence image. We employed a differential excitation protocol to extract high spatial frequency information. By modulating the power of the excitation laser, the spatial resolution of imaging was enhanced approximately 1.6 times compared to confocal microscopy. Given the straightforwardness of the experimental setup and data processing, we anticipate that this method can improve the spatial resolution of sensing and biological labeling with defects in solids.

We investigate the connection between two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup $g$ of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface $x_m$ in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial $w_m$. The first construction, the Berglund-H\"ubsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, utilizes another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface $x_{m^t}$ inside it by a dual group $g^t$. In the second construction, the Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide a straightforward evidence of the equivalence of these two constructions."

At 4:30 PM, my best friend Eric and his son Nunu came over with a huge pan of baked ziti. The tables were set, and I had all the food I cooked ready, and the bells were ringing like crazy. Before I could blink, my best friend from Florida walked through the door, and that made me smile. I missed that guy so much. The bonus was him bringing his wife along. She is expecting, and this is the closest I could get to being an uncle. I spoiled her with tray after tray of food and tiramisu.

Menstruation. A simple word describing a natural biological process, weighed down by centuries of stigma, has been transformed into something most of us can only speak about in whispers. But why? As I speak to you right now, more than 800 million women around the world are having a period. None of us would exist without it, and yet it remains an "embarrassing" subject to broach. From my experience and that of the women around me, I can tell you that it's exhausting. It's exhausting to carefully take out a brown paper bag hiding a pad, stuffing it into your pocket in the middle of a class and rushing to the washroom as discreetly as possible. It's exhausting to sit through lessons and meetings pretending to be absolutely normal, while internally crying out from intense period cramps.

Paris's promise raises Welsh hopes has seen a better Six Nations match than Saturday's epic in Paris? And can the Welsh revival continue all the way to a first Grand Slam since 1978? Those are the two questions occupying not just Wales supporters but rugby fans as a whole after a scintillating display in Paris. Welsh legend Mervyn Davies, a member of two of three Grand Slam-winning sides of the 1970s, hailed it as "one of the great performances of the past three decades." Martyn Williams, Wales' two-try scorer on the day, called it "one of the most surreal games I have ever played in." A crestfallen France coach, Bernard Laporte, simply observed  "There was a French half and there was a Welsh half."

Sun Microsystems has introduced a new pay-as-you-go service, allowing customers to rent significant computing power on an hourly basis. The Sun Grid service charges users $1 (53p) per hour for access to processing and storage power on Sun-managed systems. This concept of grid computing represents a modern trend, likened to the evolution of electricity, positioning computing capacity as a critical commodity alongside hardware and software. Jonathan Schwartz, Sun's Chief Operating Officer, highlighted this parallel, suggesting grid computing might evolve similarly to essential utilities like electricity and water. "Why build your own grid when you can use ours for just a dollar an hour?" he posed during a webcast at Sun's quarterly Network Computing event in California.

Home phones face an unclear future; the fixed-line phone in your home could soon be an endangered species. Research by handset maker Nokia shows that more and more people are using their mobile phone for every call they make or take. According to the study, more than 45 million people in the UK, Germany, the US, and South Korea now only use a mobile. It showed that people keep their fixed-line phone because call charges are lower, but most of those questioned said the future was definitely mobile. The Nokia-sponsored research showed that mobiles and fixed phones were used for different purposes. Home phones were used for longer calls, but conversations on mobiles tended to be shorter, between mobiles and to friends. In the UK, 69% of those questioned said they turned to their fixed phone because it was still cheaper to use than a mobile.

Baryonic feedback effects lead to a dampening of the weak lensing angular power spectrum on smaller scales. The uncertain shape and magnitude of this damping pose significant challenges for forthcoming cosmological weak lensing surveys, such as those conducted by Euclid or LSST. In this first installment of a two-part series, we utilize simulations to construct a Euclid-like tomographic mock dataset for the cosmic shear power spectrum and the associated covariance matrix, both of which are adjusted for baryonic influences using the baryonification method outlined by Schneider et al. (2019). Additionally, we have developed an emulator to provide rapid predictions of baryonic power suppression, enabling us to perform a likelihood inference analysis for a standard ΛCDM cosmology, incorporating both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

A novel approach to mitigating the adverse effects of selfish routing on network efficiency in autonomous vehicle systems is presented, building upon the concept of routing controllability. However, the conventional assumption that autonomous vehicles would willingly submit to external control by a central authority is overly simplistic. This paper introduces a pioneering joint routing and pricing control framework, designed to motivate autonomous vehicles to adopt system-optimal routing strategies by offering toll savings, while simultaneously imposing congestion charges on user equilibrium-seeking autonomous vehicles and human-driven vehicles. This complex problem is formulated as a bi-level optimization paradigm, wherein dynamic tolls are optimized at the upper level, and a mixed equilibrium simulation-based dynamic traffic assignment model is employed at the lower level to account for the interactions between autonomous and human-driven vehicles in a mixed fleet environment.

Following the suggestion from Monte-Carlo experiments in Jiménez (J. Turbul., 2020), which indicate that dipoles are as crucial to the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence as individual vortex cores, it is discovered that the kinetic energy of this flow is primarily carried by elongated streams formed through the concatenation of dipoles. Vortices separate into two distinct families: small, fast-moving cores and larger, slowly moving ones that can be described as 'frozen' into a slowly evolving 'crystal'. The kinematics of these two families exhibit significant differences, with only the former displaying self-similarity. The latter family is responsible for the majority of the flow's kinetic energy, and its vortices form the dipoles and streams. The mechanisms driving the growth of this slow component are discussed.

The tails of diboson production at the LHC are sensitive to the interference between the Standard Model and higher-dimension operators parameterizing the effects of heavy new physics. However, helicity selection rules for diboson scattering amplitudes set an obstruction to the naïve interference contributions of dimension-six operators, causing the leading contribution to the total diboson rate correction to cancel. In this case, carefully measuring the azimuthal decay angles "resurrects" the interference, restoring sensitivity to the "non-interfering" operators. We explore these signatures in detail and find that the EFT uncertainties associated with higher-dimensional operators are uniquely well-suppressed by constructing an asymmetry variable generated exclusively by these non-interfering operators, relegating the effects of higher-dimensional, interfering operators to the same status as statistical errors in this observable.

Hello! I watched "Troy" with my family tonight, and overall, I wasn't very impressed. I'm too tired to go into detail, but I felt the movie had potential. Khyatt (non-privileged).pst El Paso tests market for mainline expansion. El Paso Natural Gas apparently got the message when its last open season for 1.2 Bcf/d of firm transportation capacity attracted bids for 14.4 Bcf/d. The pipeline has announced an open season to test market interest in a mainline expansion and to satisfy regulatory concerns that something needs to be done to relieve the apparent capacity constraint at the California border. El Paso has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to enormous demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a Jan. 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" now facing the California gas market.

Voters flock to blog awards site; voting is under way for the annual Bloggies, which recognise the best web blogs - online spaces where people publish their thoughts - of the year. Nominations were announced on Sunday, but traffic to the official site was so heavy that the website was temporarily closed due to too many visitors. Weblogs have been nominated in 30 categories, from the top regional blog to the best-kept-secret blog. Blogs had a huge year, with a top US dictionary naming "blog" the word of 2004. Technorati, a blog search engine, tracks about six million blogs and says that more than 12,000 are added daily. A blog is created every 5.8 seconds, according to US research think-tank Pew Internet and American Life, but less than 40% of the total are updated at least once every two months.

Optomechanics and electromechanics have enabled the preparation of macroscopic mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground states, in quadrature squeezed states, and in entangled states of motion. The ability to coax larger and more tangible objects into a regime of quantum behavior has encouraged ideas for using mechanical oscillators in the processing and communication of quantum information and as precision force sensors operating beyond the standard quantum limit. However, the linear interaction between motion and light or electricity precludes access to the broader class of quantum states of motion, such as cat states or energy squeezed states. Early optomechanical proposals suggested the possibility of escaping this restriction by creating strong quadratic coupling of motion to light. Although there have been experimental demonstrations of quadratically coupled optomechanical systems, these have not yet accessed non-classical states of motion.

In 2004, Prince reigned supreme as the highest-earning musician, surpassing industry icons like Madonna and Elton John, according to *Rolling Stone* magazine's annual rankings. The legendary artist amassed an impressive $56.5 million (£30.4 million) through his *Musicology* tour, album sales, and publishing royalties. Madonna secured the second spot with $54.9 million (£29.5 million), fueled by her ambitious *Re-Invention* world tour. Meanwhile, the iconic duo Simon & Garfunkel claimed tenth place, generating $24.9 million (£13.4 million) from their highly anticipated reunion tour.  

*Rolling Stone* noted Prince's triumphant return to the spotlight after years of relative obscurity in the mainstream music scene. His 2004 tour alone grossed $90.3 million (£48.5 million) in ticket sales, while his latest album, *Musicology*, sold 1.9 million copies, cementing his resurgence as a dominant force in the industry.

Online reviews provide customers with valuable product evaluations, but these evaluations can be manipulated by professional spammers using increasingly sophisticated and insidious spamming strategies. These strategies are difficult to detect, as they can evolve rapidly, vary across spammers and products, and often go undetected. Most existing detectors focus solely on detection accuracy, which does not align with the goal of maintaining the trustworthiness of product evaluations. To address these challenges, we formulate a game-theoretic approach where spammers and detectors compete in a minimax game, leading to stable detectors that are resilient to any mixed detection strategies.

In this paper, we investigate the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of Gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to non-convexity, there exist multiple local minima that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We demonstrate that all local minima share the same form of structure, which partially identifies the component centers of the true mixture. Specifically, each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple Gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single Gaussian to multiple true components. Our results apply to the setting where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition, and remain valid even when the number of components is over- or under-specified. For Gaussian mixtures with three components, we obtain sharper results in terms of the scaling between the components, with a notable improvement in the separation between the components.

At a glance: Tory health checks  
The UK's opposition Conservative Party has announced plans to implement mandatory health screenings for immigrants if they win the upcoming general election. Here’s an overview of the proposal:  

- Individuals relocating to Britain from outside the EU for work or residence would be subject to health checks.  
- Those intending to stay for six months or longer, originating from countries with high tuberculosis (TB) rates, would require a chest X-ray and additional tests if necessary.  
- All non-EU nationals seeking to remain in the UK for a year or more would undergo a comprehensive medical examination, covering conditions such as tuberculosis, hepatitis B, and HIV.  
- A positive TB test would result in an automatic visa rejection.  
- Other medical conditions would be assessed individually on a case-by-case basis.

Entanglement is a crucial resource for processing quantum information, as it enables the manipulation of quantum states. One widely employed tool for detecting entanglement is the entanglement witness, which guarantees a positive measurement outcome for separable states and can yield a negative result for certain entangled states. However, as the Hilbert space dimension exponentially increases with respect to the system size, constructing an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states becomes increasingly challenging. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme necessitates $n+1$ local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/2$ white noise. In contrast, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states requires only two local measurement settings and can withstand up to $1/3$ white noise, demonstrating a significant reduction in the number of measurement settings required for entanglement detection.

We establish natural splittings for the values of global Mackey functors at orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups. In particular, the restriction homomorphisms between the orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups of adjacent dimensions are naturally split epimorphisms. The interest in these splittings arises from equivariant stable homotopy theory. The equivariant stable homotopy groups of every global spectrum form a global Mackey functor, so the splittings imply that certain long exact homotopy group sequences split into short exact sequences. For the real and complex global Thom spectra $\mathbf{mo}$ and $\mathbf{mu}$, the splittings imply the regularity of various Euler classes related to the tautological representations of $O(n)$ and $U(n)$.

At the time, Davies openly expressed frustration over his treatment before ultimately making the move to England, where he has since revitalized his career.

On October 4, 1957, the world watched in awe and fear as the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the world's first man-made satellite, into space. This little metal ball, smaller than two feet in diameter, ignited a space race between the U.S. and U.S.S.R. that would last for eighteen years and change the world as we know it. Sputnik was not actually the first piece of human technology to enter space. That distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, used by Germany in missile attacks against Allied cities as a last-ditch effort in the final years of World War II. It wasn't very effective, but by the war's end, both the U.S. and U.S.S.R. had captured the technology—along with the scientists who developed it—and began using them for their own projects.

We introduce a novel Skyrme-like conserved current in the effective theory of pions and vector mesons based on the idea of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is equivalent to the Skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. Additionally, there exist singular configurations that can be identified as \(n_f=1\) baryons charged under the new symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons serve as the Chern-Simons vector fields residing on the quantum Hall droplet that forms the \(n_f=1\) baryon. We propose that this current is the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal provides a unified picture for the two types of baryons and allows them to continuously transform into one another naturally. Furthermore, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a result of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

Low-symmetry 2D materials, such as res$_2$ and rese$_2$ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, group-IV monochalcogenide monolayers, borophene, and others, exhibit more complex atomic structures compared to the honeycomb lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. The reduced symmetries of these emerging materials lead to inhomogeneous electron, optical, valley, and spin responses, as well as novel properties such as ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, large nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Additionally, novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations can occur due to low symmetry. The "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" special topic highlights recent experimental and theoretical research on these emerging materials.

Brizzel to Run AAA's in Sheffield

Ballymena sprinter Paul Brizzel will be among eight of Ireland's European indoor hopefuls competing in this weekend's AAA Championships. US-based Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll are the only Irish athletes selected so far for the Europeans who will not run in Sheffield. Brizzel will defend his 200m title in the British trials. In-form James McIlroy will aim to confirm his place in the British team for Madrid by winning the 800m title. McIlroy has been in tremendous form on the European circuit in recent weeks. He is one of the fastest 800m runners in the world this winter and already seems assured of a place in Madrid. Corkman Mark Carroll confirmed in midweek that he would join Cragg in the European Championships. Carroll is ranked number three in the world 3000m ranking at the moment, with Cragg occupying the top spot.

Acclaimed actor Kevin Spacey's latest cinematic endeavour, "Beyond the Sea", made its UK debut in London's iconic Leicester Square, drawing in a star-studded crowd. The film, a poignant tribute to the life of 1950s music legend Bobby Darin, marks a triple triumph for Spacey, who not only stars in the title role but also wrote and directed the movie. In a remarkable display of his vocal prowess, Spacey performs all 18 songs featured on the film's soundtrack, a testament to his admiration for Darin's remarkable talent. Joining Spacey on the red carpet was his co-star, Kate Bosworth, who brings to life the role of Sandra Dee, Darin's wife and a renowned Hollywood actress in her own right. Bosworth, who attended the premiere with her British partner Orlando Bloom, confessed to having been unfamiliar with Darin's work prior to the film, but has since become an ardent fan.

Iraq Announces Plans to Expand Nationwide Telecommunications Coverage through Competitive Bidding Process

In a bid to revolutionize its telecommunications sector, the Iraqi Ministry of Communications has announced plans to invite bids for two telephone licenses, aiming to significantly enhance nationwide coverage over the next decade. The ministry has extended an invitation to local, Arab, and foreign companies to participate in the bidding process, with the winning bidder set to form a strategic partnership with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC).

The successful bidder will be responsible for designing, installing, and operating a comprehensive fixed phone network, offering a range of services including voice, fax, and internet connectivity. This ambitious initiative aims to address the country's current low telephone service penetration rate of approximately 4.5%, with a target of increasing this to 25% within the next 10 years.

The ministry envisions the development of a dynamic and highly visible telecommunications sector, capable of driving economic growth and social development. To facilitate the bidding process, the ministry will publish detailed information on its website on February 9, while also hosting a roadshow for investors in Amman, Jordan, to showcase the opportunities and potential of Iraq's burgeoning telecommunications market.

Voters flock to blog awards site as voting gets underway for the annual Bloggies, which recognize the best web blogs - online spaces where individuals publish their thoughts - of the year. Nominations were announced on Sunday, but the official site experienced a temporary closure due to an overwhelming influx of visitors. Weblogs have been nominated in 30 categories, ranging from the top regional blog to the best-kept-secret blog. Blogs had a remarkable year, with a top US dictionary naming "blog" the word of 2004. In this work, a generalization of human gait studies was developed from existing models in the literature, including those by Keller and Kockshenev. This hybrid model combines a metabolic energy minimization strategy with a nonlinear description of the mass center's libration movement, aiming to replicate the walk-run transition behavior. Experimental data analysis across various speed regimes revealed that the perimeter of the mass center's trajectory is a significant quantity in quantifying this dynamic. An experimental procedure was implemented in collaboration with the research group in biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratories at the Manuela Beltrán University in Bogotá, Colombia.

A novel approach to modifying the surface electrical conductivity of ceramics has been successfully demonstrated through metal ion implantation, a technique that has been effectively utilized in various applications. Notably, this method enables the precise control of voltage distribution across high-voltage insulators, thereby significantly enhancing their operational voltage capacity without compromising their integrity through surface flashover. However, a critical consideration in the implementation of this technique is the long-term stability of the conductivity induced by metal ion implantation, particularly when the implanted metal species is prone to oxidation. This study presents an in-depth examination of the long-term behavior of titanium-implanted alumina, revealing that while an initial decline of up to 40% in conductivity occurs within the first few weeks following implantation, the subsequent decrease is remarkably gradual, with a mere 10% reduction observed over the ensuing year.

Jamieson Issues Warning to Bigots Scotland's justice minister has warned bigoted soccer fans that she intends to hit them "where it hurts most" by banning them from matches. Cathy Jamieson said exclusion orders are among a series of measures being considered in the Scottish Executive's campaign against sectarianism. She praised Celtic and Rangers for their efforts in addressing the problem. However, the minister stated that stopping sectarian abuse associated with Old Firm matches is a key objective. Ms. Jamieson was speaking ahead of the third-round Scottish Cup clash between the Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The sectarianism long associated with certain sections of the fanbase from both clubs has become a significant target for the Executive. Last week, Ms. Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell met with supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the issue.

Orientational dynamics in the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer, featuring both mesogenic and functional side groups, were investigated using the Kerr effect and dielectric spectroscopy. Notably, this study revealed that, unlike low-molecular-weight mesogens, the relaxation of electric birefringence in a mesogenic polymer melt above the nematic-isotropic phase transition temperature can be described by a sum of multiple exponential processes, with two dominant processes playing a crucial role. These primary processes replace each other within a temperature range of approximately fifty degrees. Furthermore, dielectric spectroscopy enabled the identification of two distinct orientational relaxation processes: one attributed to the rotation of side mesogenic groups, and the other associated with the motion of main chain segments.

While it may seem an obvious statement, I began pondering this issue about 12 years ago, initially in the context of developing countries. However, considering the global scope, I believe it's essential to recognize that every country on the planet has areas where good teachers are sorely needed. If you envision a world map, I think you'll agree that for each country, you could draw circles around regions where the need for quality education is most pressing. Moreover, these are often the very areas from which the greatest challenges arise. We have an intriguing paradox - good educators are often reluctant to venture to places where they are needed most urgently. In 1999, I embarked on an experiment to address this conundrum, starting with a simple initiative in New Delhi.

Yukos seeks court action in the US on Wednesday to seek sanctions against Baikal Finance Group, the little-known firm that has acquired its main asset. Yukos has stated it will sue Baikal and others involved in the sale of Yuganskneftegas for $20 billion in damages. Yukos' US lawyers will attempt to have Baikal assets frozen after the Russian government ignored a US court order last week blocking the sale. Weight pruning has been widely acknowledged as a straightforward and effective method for eliminating redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving acceleration on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques essentially involve trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a new sparsity dimension, namely pattern-based sparsity, which comprises pattern and connectivity sparsity, and achieves both high accuracy and hardware friendliness. With carefully designed patterns, the proposed pruning method consistently and unprecedentedly achieves accuracy enhancement and better feature extraction ability across different DNN structures and datasets. Our pattern-aware pruning framework also enables simultaneous pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern and connectivity pruning, and weight training. Our approach, based on the new pattern-based sparsity, naturally fits into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

According to a two-year study, women are increasingly taking charge of protecting their home computers from malicious internet attacks, with the number of women purchasing programs to safeguard PCs from viruses, spam, and spyware attacks rising by 11.2% each year between 2002 and 2004. The study, conducted by a leading internet security firm, reveals that security messages are reaching a diverse range of internet users. It is believed that 40% of those purchasing home network security software are retired individuals, a figure that has increased by an average of 13.2% over the past three years. Moreover, more retired women (53%) are buying security software than retired men, reflecting the shifting demographics and growing awareness of the greater risks that high-speed broadband internet connections pose to internet users.

Cabinet anger at Brown cash raid. Ministers are unhappy about plans to use Whitehall cash to keep council tax bills down, Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford has acknowledged. Gordon Brown reallocated £512m from central to local government budgets in his pre-budget report on Thursday. Mr Raynsford said he had held some "pretty frank discussions" with fellow ministers over the plans. But he said local governments had to deliver good services without big council tax rises. The central government cash is part of a £1bn package to help local authorities in England keep next year's council tax rises below 5%, in what is likely to be a general election year. Mr Raynsford said nearly all central government departments had an interest in well-run local authorities.

We propose a novel skyrmion-like conserved current in the effective theory of pions and vector mesons based on the idea of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is equivalent to the skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. In addition, there exist singular configurations that can be identified as $n_f=1$ baryons charged under the new symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons play the role of the chern-Simons vector fields living on the quantum Hall droplet that forms the $n_f=1$ baryon. We suggest that this current is the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal provides a unified picture for the two types of baryons and allows them to continuously transform one into the other in a natural way. Furthermore, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a result of Seiberg-like dualities between gluons and vector mesons.

Manchester United defeated Manchester City 1-0 in a scrappy match to move within nine points of the Premier League leaders. Wayne Rooney scored the only goal of the game with a low shot that deflected off Richard Dunne and into the net. Seven minutes later, Dunne hooked a volley over David James' head and into his own net. Steve McManaman wasted City's best chance in the first half when he shot wide from three yards out. In the opening 45 minutes, United looked unlikely to earn the win they needed to keep their title hopes alive, as their approach play was more labored than patient and they managed only one chance, a Paul Scholes header over the bar.

The starving orphan seeking a second helping of gruel. The spinster wasting away in her tattered wedding dress. The stone-hearted miser plagued by the ghost of Christmas past. More than a century after his death, these remain recognizable figures from the work of Charles Dickens. So striking is his body of work that it gave rise to its own adjective. But what are the features of Dickens's writing that make it so special? Dickens’s fiction brims with anticipation through brooding settings, plot twists, and mysteries. These features of his work kept his audience wanting more. When first published, his stories were serialized, meaning they were released a few chapters at a time in affordable literary journals and only later reprinted as books.

We introduce a novel mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator for planetary systems, capable of resolving close encounters with precision. Our approach is founded on a time-regularization technique that preserves the stability properties of symplectic integrators while adaptively reducing the step size during close encounters between planets. By employing a high-order MVS scheme, we enable the integration of large time steps, even in the presence of distant close encounters. We demonstrate the efficacy of this algorithm in resolving almost exact collisions, with a mutual separation of a fraction of the physical radius, using the same time-step as in weakly perturbed systems, such as the solar system. Our long-term simulations of six super-Earths experiencing strong scattering over 50,000 years reveal the stability and accuracy of our approach. We compare our method to hybrid methods like Mercury and show that, for an equivalent computational cost, we achieve significantly better energy conservation.

Deep hashing techniques have demonstrated exceptional performance in large-scale image retrieval systems, achieving remarkable accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency. A central challenge in these methods lies in the effective optimization of discrete binary codes. Current approaches typically employ activation functions such as sigmoid(·) or tanh(·), coupled with quantization loss minimization to approximate discrete values. Unfortunately, this conventional framework presents a critical limitation: an increasing number of hash bits become trapped in suboptimal saturation regions of the activation functions, unable to recover. We identify this phenomenon as the "dead bits problem" (DBP). Moreover, standard quantization losses inadvertently exacerbate this issue. To address this challenge, we introduce an innovative yet straightforward gradient amplification mechanism that operates prior to activation functions, effectively mitigating DBP while preserving model performance.
