This was a productive weekend. I indulged in plenty of 'god's green leaf' (a hobby I should have taken up sooner), listened to a wealth of 'nevermore' (a pleasure I should have prioritized earlier), and devoured three books: Bruce Sterling's 'Heavy Weather', Larry Niven's 'World of Ptavvs', and Octavia Butler's 'Parable of the Sower'. 'Heavy Weather' was a gripping cyberpunk tale that explored a dystopian near-future America, ravaged by global warming and chaos. While the story was cleverly crafted and paced, I failed to connect with the characters or glean any profound insights into the narrative. Not that they weren't well-developed and vividly rendered - it's a solid book, but not an exceptional one, I reckon.

I was initially skeptical about this game before I played it. I wasn't even sure about purchasing it. The portal gun and portal aspect seemed unusual to me, even though I appreciate science fiction. Boy, I was wrong. The humor and voice acting in this game are phenomenal. They're not cheesy, except for a few instances. The various characters have their own unique sense of humor. Their distinct personalities really shine through. The level design is just fantastic. By the way, I never played the original Portal game. One of these days, I'm planning to play it if I can spare the time. I wouldn't have played it if I hadn't played the sequel.

Where do I start with this absolutely beautiful game? Its story is utterly charming, packed with so much detail—it's a bundle of happiness, bad puns, annoying dogs, and secrets. It knows when to be serious but isn’t afraid of funny moments. The character design is spot-on, featuring a diverse cast with unique personalities and hidden depths. It stands out from other RPGs because you don’t have to kill anyone—a rare concept in the genre. Drawing heavy inspiration from EarthBound, one of the best RPGs I’ve ever played, it’s clear this game is something special. Plus, it easily has the best soundtrack I’ve heard in years.

Sid Meier's Civilization: Beyond Earth is a turn-based game where you build your civilization to achieve a specific goal or "victory." In previous games, you were set on Earth's timeline, going through periods like the Stone Age, Medieval Age, and Modern Age; however, in this game, you are set in the future on a mission to colonize a new planet. What I enjoy about this game is that 2K took a new approach and added some new mechanics, such as the affinity system and orbital layer. What I didn't enjoy was that these new mechanics could make the game overwhelming at times. I think one improvement could be the incorporation of new artwork, like the environments you experience when communicating with another leader.

Do I even need to explain why Portal is amazing? Portal is a puzzle game; it gives you a concept and then gets out. It's an amazing way to test your mind while still being fun. Although this game isn't as story-heavy, it did give birth to one of the most popular games of 2011. I still play this game and beat it when I'm feeling bored, and I love to come back and see what gave birth to a legend and what is a legend. Gameplay: Portal's gameplay is quite simple to explain, at least. You use portals to your advantage, along with buttons that activate a part of the puzzle. There are two buttons in the game: one that you press and one that requires a cube to be placed on it.

I'm feeling uncomfortable with the way investigative reporting is portrayed in this article. As someone who has grown up in a country where questioning authority is not encouraged, I find it difficult to fully understand and empathize with the passion and angst behind it. While I appreciate the importance of investigative reporting, I cannot help but feel that the consequences of pursuing such reporting are not entirely justifiable. It's not something I'm comfortable with, and I don't think I can fully grasp the full extent of the issue at hand. Perhaps it's because I don't fully believe in it, or maybe it's because I'm not used to questioning things out loud. In any case, I find it hard to relate to the idea of questioning authority without fear of reprisal.

GOP gets scolded on recruitment; naturalization ceremonies in Jacksonville are to be relocated after complaints that GOP activists registered new citizens as Republicans. By Gary Fineout and Alfonso Chardy, the citizenship ceremony in Jacksonville seemed to go off as usual, with a crowd of nearly 200 people going home as new Americans. Just before the new citizens left the June 29 event, an immigration official directing the swearing-in urged them to stop by a voter registration table - a not uncommon sight at naturalization ceremonies. But this table was unusual: those handing out forms were Republican volunteers, and the party affiliation box had been checked off ahead of time to make all of the new voters members of the GOP. All of it was suspicious to Linda Cross, who was there to watch her husband, Dario Cruz, take his citizenship oath.

Captains line up for aid match. Ireland's Brian O'Driscoll is one of four Six Nations captains included in the Northern Hemisphere squad for the IRB Rugby Aid match on 5 March. France's Fabien Pelous, Gordon Bullock of Scotland, and Italy's Marco Bortolami are also in the Northern party. Sir Clive Woodward will coach the Northern team against Rod MacQueen's Southern Hemisphere team in a tsunami fund-raising match at Twickenham. "I'm looking forward to working with such outstanding players," he said. It will be a chance for Woodward to assess some of his options before unveiling his British and Irish Lions touring party, who will visit New Zealand in the summer. "The game promises to be a great spectacle," he said.

Scotland's Justice Minister, Jamie Jamieson, has warned that she wants to take a tough stance against bigoted soccer fans, warning that she intends to ban them from matches "where it hurts the most" if they engage in sectarian behavior. This is part of a series of measures being considered by the Scottish government to tackle the issue of sectarianism. Jamieson praised Celtic and Rangers for their efforts in addressing the problem, emphasizing that stopping sectarian abuse associated with old firm matches is a key objective. The minister was speaking ahead of the third round of the Scottish Cup clash between Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The sectarianism long associated with sections of the support from both clubs has become a significant target for the executive. Last week, Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnie met with supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the issue.

On October 4, 1957, the world observed with a mix of amazement and apprehension as the Soviet Union successfully launched Sputnik, the first artificial satellite, into space. This small metallic orb, less than two feet in diameter, ignited a space race between the United States and the Soviet Union that endured for eighteen years and profoundly transformed our world. However, Sputnik was not the first human-made object to reach space—that distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, which Germany employed in missile attacks against Allied cities during the closing years of World War II. Although the V-2 wasn't particularly effective, by the war's end, both the U.S. and the U.S.S.R. had seized the technology and the engineers behind it, utilizing them for their own burgeoning aerospace projects.

A novel, conserved current is introduced within the framework of an effective theory encompassing pions and vector mesons, grounded in the concept of hidden local symmetry. This current's associated charge is demonstrated to be equivalent to the skyrmion charge for all smooth configurations, while singular configurations can be identified as baryons with n_f=1, exhibiting charge under the newly proposed symmetry. Notably, vector mesons assume the role of Chern-Simons vector fields residing on the quantum Hall droplet that constitutes the n_f=1 baryon. We propose that this current provides the accurate effective representation of the baryon current at low energies, thereby offering a unified paradigm for the two distinct types of baryons. This unified framework enables a seamless transformation between the two baryon types, while Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a manifestation of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons, fostering a deeper understanding of the intricate relationships between these fundamental particles.

This study shows that the eccentric debris rings around the stars Fomalhaut and HD 202628 are narrower than expected in the standard eccentric planet perturbation scenario, also known as the pericenter glow. The standard scenario assumes an initially circular and narrow belt of planetesimals at semi-major axis a, whose eccentricity is increased to ef after the gas disc has dispersed due to secular perturbations from an eccentric planet, resulting in a belt of width 2ae_f. A minor modification to this scenario suggests that narrower belts can arise if the planetesimals are initially eccentric, which could result from earlier planet perturbations during the gas-rich protoplanetary disc phase. Alternatively, a primordial eccentricity could be caused by instabilities that increase the disc eccentricity, without the need for any planets.

I am a connoisseur of culture in all its forms. My daily commute is soundtracked by an eclectic playlist—from the grandeur of Wagner and Mozart to the pop brilliance of Christina Aguilera, the soulful twang of Josh Turner, and the raw energy of Kirk Franklin’s gospel rap. Symphonies, concertos, and an ever-expanding library of music accompany me everywhere.  

My literary appetite is equally insatiable. I traverse the spectrum from Ian McEwan’s profound narratives to Stephanie Meyer’s addictive sagas—yes, I’ve journeyed through the entire *Twilight* series.  

At home, my sanctuary is my home theater, where I indulge in a feast of visual storytelling: DVDs, on-demand films, and television series. Whether it’s the gripping drama of *Law & Order: SVU*, the razor-sharp wit of Tina Fey in *30 Rock*, or the unfiltered justice of *Judge Judy*—where real cases meet final rulings—I immerse myself in it all.

Have you ever experienced that surreal moment of introspection, where your mind drifts from mundane reality to profound existential questions? Picture this: you're trapped in a monotonous math lecture, your consciousness detaching as you ponder the very mechanisms of thought that allow you to comprehend equations. In this strange epiphany, you become acutely aware of your own existence—here, now, in this classroom—and question the meaning behind it all. The sheer wonder of being alive washes over you... only to be undercut by the crushing realization that such philosophical musings during basic algebra are frankly ridiculous.  

And while we're discussing academic grievances, let's address the true atrocity: block scheduling. Whoever devised this torturous system clearly never endured the agony of a 150-minute lecture on any subject. It's nothing short of institutionalized cruelty.

Indeed, for 20 years, I worked in finance. I knew everything about finance, for sure. I knew the global economy because it was my life. All the topics that you could bring up about ecology, the end of the world, etc., that I knew like the back of my hand, I didn't really believe in them. In 2007, an extremely unusual event occurred, you may remember - the subprime crisis, the collapse of big American banks. It was an electric shock. I had a fair few stormy conversations with my wife about the matter, about my job, and the limits of the model. Besides the crisis, I realized that I was completely wrong, that we were heading for disaster.

Menstruation, a straightforward term for a natural biological process, has been burdened by centuries of stigma, forcing many of us to discuss it in hushed tones. But why is that the case? As I speak to you now, over 800 million women worldwide are experiencing their periods. This process is essential to human existence, yet it continues to be an "embarrassing" topic. From my experiences and those of the women around me, I can tell you it's draining. It's exhausting to discreetly pull a pad in a brown paper bag from your bag, tuck it into your pocket during class, and hurry to the restroom hoping not to draw attention. It's tiring to endure lessons and meetings, feigning normalcy while inwardly battling intense period cramps.

U2, the Irish band, have been honored at France's biggest music awards, but were beaten to a prize by the boy band Blue. U2 received a special achievement prize at the NRJ music awards, but Blue beat them to the international group award. U2 was named the best new international artist, and took the best international song title for 'Beautiful Day.' More than five million radio listeners voted in the awards. The international male and female prizes went to Usher and Avril Lavigne respectively. Collecting his band's award from model Naomi Campbell at the Cannes ceremony, U2 frontman Bono said in French  'I'm not from this country, but I'll make a little confession to you - it's at the Côte d'Azur I feel at home.'

Entanglement plays a vital role in quantum information processing. A commonly used method to identify entanglement is through an entanglement witness, where measuring the witness operator is always positive for separable states but can turn negative for certain entangled states. However, as the system size increases, the dimension of the Hilbert space expands exponentially, making it particularly challenging to design an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states. When dealing with $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most resilient witness approach requires $n+1$ local measurement settings and can endure up to $1/2$ white noise. In contrast, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states only necessitates two local measurement settings and can withstand up to $1/3$ white noise.

Downloads enter the US singles chart as digital music downloads are incorporated into the main US singles chart for the first time. Billboard's revamped Hot 100 chart now combines data from sales of music downloads, previously tracked on a separate download chart, with physical singles sales and airplay information. Green Day's 'Boulevard of Broken Dreams' currently holds the number two spot on Billboard's pop chart and tops the digital chart. The UK singles chart is also set to include download sales later this year. In the US, digital sales are already used to compile Billboard's Hot Digital Sales chart, and will now be merged with physical singles sales and airplay data to form the new Hot 100 chart. Additionally, Billboard's new Pop 100 chart combines airplay, digital, and physical sales, but limits its airplay information to US radio stations that play chart music.

During my career as a palliative care specialist, I experienced a profoundly humbling moment that remains etched in my memory. Several years ago, I was consulted to evaluate a distinguished retired English professor in her seventies battling pancreatic cancer. She presented with severe pain, persistent nausea, and frequent vomiting. As we discussed symptom management during our consultation, she posed a thoughtful question: did I believe medical cannabis could alleviate her suffering? This inquiry prompted me to reflect on my medical education - a sobering realization, as I suddenly recognized the complete absence of any formal training on medicinal marijuana applications in patient care. The stark gap in my knowledge became painfully apparent in that moment.

The world's largest and most devastating environmental and industrial project is situated in the heart of the largest and most intact forest in the world, Canada's boreal forest. It stretches right across northern Canada; in Labrador, it's home to the largest remaining wild caribou herd in the world: the George River caribou herd, numbering approximately 400,000 animals. Unfortunately, when I was there, I couldn't find one of them, but you have the antlers as proof. All across the boreal, we're blessed with this incredible abundance of wetlands. Wetlands, globally, are one of the most endangered ecosystems. They're absolutely critical ecosystems—they clean air, they clean water, they sequester large amounts of greenhouse gases, and they're home to a huge diversity of species.

The digital landscape buzzes with excitement as voting commences for the prestigious Bloggies Awards, honoring this year's most outstanding weblogs—dynamic online platforms where creators share their perspectives with the world. Following Sunday's nomination reveal, overwhelming public interest caused the official website to temporarily crash due to unprecedented traffic volumes.  

This year's competition features weblogs across 30 diverse categories, ranging from top regional blogs to hidden gems awaiting discovery. The blogging phenomenon reached new heights in 2004, with "blog" earning recognition as Word of the Year by a leading U.S. dictionary authority.  

The exponential growth of the blogosphere is undeniable: Technorati, a specialized blog search engine, currently monitors approximately six million active blogs, with over 12,000 new entries emerging daily. Research from Pew Internet and American Life reveals a new blog springs to life every 5.8 seconds, though fewer than 40% maintain regular updates at least bimonthly. This surge underscores blogging's transformative impact on modern digital culture.

The article discusses how Scotland's Justice Minister, Cathy Jamieson, has warned bigoted soccer fans that she wants to hit them "where it hurts most" by banning them from matches. The article states that exclusion orders are one of a series of measures being considered in the Scottish Executive campaign against sectarianism. The minister praised Celtic and Rangers for their work in tackling the problem, but noted that stopping sectarian abuse associated with old firm matches is a key objective. The article also mentions that Ms. Jamieson was speaking ahead of the third round of Scottish Cup clashes between the Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The sectarianism long associated with sections of the support from both clubs has become a significant target for the executive. Last week, Ms. Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell met with supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the issue.

You’ve been accused of a crime you did not commit. It’s impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist that you’re innocent, you’ll likely be found guilty and executed. However, if you confess, apologize, and implicate others, you’ll be set free. Do you provide a false confession or risk a public hanging? This was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of Salem, Massachusetts between February 1692 and May 1693. They were victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor, and a justice system that valued repentance over truth. Salem was settled in 1626 by Puritans, a group of English Protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of Salem, with battles against their Native American neighbors and groups of French settlers being commonplace.

Man City 0-2 Man Utd. Manchester United reduced Chelsea's Premiership lead to nine points after a scrappy victory over Manchester City. Wayne Rooney met Gary Neville's cross to the near post with a low shot, which went in via a deflection off Richard Dunne, to put United ahead. Seven minutes later, the unfortunate Dunne hooked a volley over David James' head and into his own net. Steve McManaman wasted City's best chance when he shot wide from three yards in the first half. In the opening 45 minutes, United had looked unlikely to earn the win they needed to maintain any chance of catching Chelsea in the title race. Their approach play was more laboured than patient, and they managed to fashion just one chance—a Paul Scholes header over the bar.

British stars denied major Oscars: Hopes of winning major Oscars for British talent were dashed as UK stars failed to secure acting and directing prizes. Despite three nominations, Mike Leigh's "Vera Drake" did not win the director or screenplay awards, and there was no prize for star Imelda Staunton. Kate Winslet also lost to Best Actress recipient Hilary Swank, while Clive Owen and Sophie Okonedo did not win Best Supporting Actor awards. The UK's Andrea Arnold, however, won the Short Film Award for her gritty drama "Wasp." Arnold, who presented children's television shows "Number 73" and "Motormouth" in the 1980s, said it was "totally overwhelming" to win. "Wasp" tells the story of a single mother living on the breadline, surpassing films by fellow UK nominees Gary McKendry and Ashvin Kumar.

A recent study inspired by Monte Carlo experiments (Jiménez, Journal of Turbulence, 2020) reveals that dipoles play a crucial role in the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence, rivaling the importance of individual vortex cores. Notably, the kinetic energy of this flow is predominantly carried by elongated streams formed through the concatenation of dipoles. The vortices in this system undergo a bifurcation, separating into two distinct families: small, rapidly moving cores and larger, slowly moving ones that become 'frozen' into a slowly evolving, crystalline structure. The kinematic properties of these two families exhibit stark differences, with only the smaller, faster-moving vortices displaying self-similarity. Conversely, the larger, slower-moving vortices are responsible for the majority of the flow's kinetic energy and are the primary constituents of the dipoles and streams. The underlying mechanisms driving the growth of this slow-moving component are explored in detail.

Deep learning has successfully solved many problems that are beyond the reach of heuristic algorithms. It has also been successfully applied in wireless communications, even though the current radio systems are well-understood and optimal algorithms exist for many tasks. While some gains have been achieved by learning individual parts of a receiver, a better approach is to jointly learn the whole receiver. This, however, often results in a challenging nonlinear problem, for which the optimal solution is difficult to implement. To address this, we propose a deep fully convolutional neural network, deeprx, which executes the whole receiver pipeline from frequency domain signal stream to uncoded bits in a 5g-compliant manner. We facilitate accurate channel estimation by constructing the input of the convolutional neural network in a very specific manner using both the data and pilot symbols.

In this paper, we investigate multi-agent discrete-event systems with partial observation. The agents can be categorized into several groups, each comprising agents with isomorphic state transition structures, and can be relabeled into a single template. Based on this template, we design a scalable supervisor whose state size and computational cost are independent of the number of agents. This scalable supervisor, under partial observation, does not require recomputation regardless of how many agents are added or removed from the system. We generalize our earlier findings on partial observation by proposing sufficient conditions for the safety and maximal permissiveness of the least restrictive supervisor on the template level. An illustrative example is provided to demonstrate the proposed scalable supervisory synthesis.

Wales 'must learn health lessons'. The new health minister for Wales says there are lessons to learn from England in tackling waiting lists. Dr Brian Gibbons, on his first full day in the job after Jane Hutt was sacked, admitted "big challenges" but insisted the "essentials" were in place. But both Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats said Dr Gibbons needed to change policy. Meanwhile, Ms Hutt defended her record, saying waiting times and lists were "only 10% of the health agenda". Dr Gibbons, who was a GP in Blaengwynfi, in the Upper Afan Valley, before becoming AM for Aberavon, said NHS staff wanted a period of consolidation after "tremendous change and reform". One of the biggest problems that had faced Ms Hutt during her five-and-a-half years as the assembly's first health minister was the length of waiting lists in Wales.

While I've experienced some minor frustrations, I've thoroughly enjoyed this game. The save system, unfortunately, is imperfect, as the risk of sudden death can be disheartening. On my first playthrough, I suffered a setback when my main character perished around the 10-hour mark, which is roughly 10% through the game. I've since acquired a third-party mod with a save feature to prevent such catastrophic failures from occurring. Additionally, the aiming reticle is slightly undersized, and the zoom functionality could be more robust. Notwithstanding these minor drawbacks, I've found the game to be an absolute blast. As someone who doesn't typically enjoy zombie-themed games, I've been pleasantly surprised by the engaging combination of strategic gameplay, mission objectives, and first-person shooter/beat-em-up mechanics. I've found myself playing it as frequently as possible, often finding myself thinking, 'Just one more mission, just one more supply run - or I'll be running away and want to get to a safe location before shutting the game down.'

Undertale is a masterpiece that transcends the sum of its parts. The graphics are commendable for a retro game, and the music is delightful. However, when combined, they create truly beautiful and impactful moments. The script masterfully ranges from comedic to dramatic to heartbreaking to hopeful, sometimes blending all of these elements simultaneously while cleverly subverting typical RPG tropes. And this is without even delving into the game's rich world, memorable characters, engrossing story, and engaging gameplay—I could easily dedicate pages to each of these aspects. Instead, I'll simply say this: if you appreciate RPGs, play this game. If you enjoy video games, play this game. If you have seven free hours at any point in your life, make sure to play this game.

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide (pcw) are reported. The pcw consists of two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. Models are developed and validated for the transduction of beam motion into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode (gm) of the pcw, for probe frequencies both far from and near the dielectric band edge. Since our pcw is designed for near-field atom trapping, this research establishes a foundation for assessing potential detrimental effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near pcw surfaces. Longer-term goals include achieving strong atom-mediated coupling between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the pcw's gms, enabling quantum-level optomechanics with atoms, photons, and phonons.

This weekend, a talented group of eight Irish athletes will represent Ireland at the European Indoor Championships in Sheffield, with sprinter Paul Brizzel among them. Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll are the only Irish athletes selected so far, with Brizzel set to defend his 200m title in the British trials. Meanwhile, James McIlroy will look to secure his place in the British team for the Europeans by winning the 800m title. McIlroy has been in exceptional form on the European circuit in recent weeks, and his speed and endurance have made him a strong contender for a spot in the Madrid team. In a mid-week confirmation, Mark Carroll revealed that he will join Cragg in the European Championships, with the duo aiming to make a strong impression on the international stage. Carroll, currently ranked third in the world 3000m ranking, will be a key player for the Irish team, alongside Cragg, who tops the world rankings.

Continuous-time quantum walks have emerged as a highly effective framework for designing various quantum algorithms. Typically, the runtime of such algorithms is determined by the quantum hitting time—the duration needed for the quantum walk to locate a target vertex with high probability. In this work, we present refined upper bounds for the quantum hitting time, applicable to numerous continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithms. Specifically, we demonstrate the utility of our approach by enhancing the hitting time upper bound for the glued-trees problem, achieving a polynomial improvement from \(o(n^5)\) to \(o(n^2\log n)\). Additionally, our techniques yield an exponential improvement in the precision dependence for the continuous-time quantum walk algorithm developed by Chakraborty et al., which identifies marked nodes in any ergodic, reversible Markov chain.

We propose a novel skyrmion-like conserved current in the effective theory of pions and vector mesons, based on the idea of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is equivalent to the Skyrme charge for any smooth configuration. In addition, we identify singular configurations that can be recognized as $n_f=1$ baryons charged under the new symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons play the role of the Chern-Simons vector fields living on the quantum Hall droplet that forms the $n_f=1$ baryon. We argue that this current is the effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal provides a unified picture for the two types of baryons and allows them to continuously transform one into the other in a natural way. Furthermore, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a result of Seiberg-like dualities between gluons and vector mesons.

The concept of money has been a fascinating subject throughout history, and I'd like to explore its evolution by examining a unique culture that existed in Micronesia during the early 1900s, known as the Yap. The Yap people are particularly intriguing due to their unique monetary system, which revolves around limestone discs called Rai stones. Unlike our modern currencies, these stones are not physically moved or exchanged, as they can grow quite massive in size. The largest Rai stone measures approximately four tons and 12 feet in diameter. To keep track of ownership, the Yap people simply keep a record of who possesses a specific portion of the stone. Interestingly, there's a story about a group of sailors who were transporting a massive stone across the ocean when they encountered some adversity, and the stone actually fell into the sea.

Yukos Heading Back to U.S. Courts: The Russian oil and gas company Yukos is due in a U.S. court on Thursday as it continues to fight for its survival. The firm is being broken up by Russian authorities to pay a $27 billion (£14 billion) tax bill. Yukos filed for bankruptcy in the U.S., aiming to use international business law to halt the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yuganskneftegaz. However, the unit was sold for $9.4 billion to state oil firm Rosneft, but only after the state auction had been disrupted. Yukos' lawyers now argue that the auction violated U.S. bankruptcy law. The company and its main shareholders have vowed to pursue any company that buys its assets, using every legal means available.

Warning over tsunami aid website: Internet users are being advised to avoid a scam website that claims to collect cash on behalf of tsunami victims. The site appears plausible because it uses an old version of the official Disasters Emergency Committee webpage. However, the DEC has no connection with the fake site and has contacted the police about it. This site is just the latest in a long list of scams attempting to exploit the goodwill generated by the tsunami disaster. The website link is contained in a spam email currently circulating. The message's subject line reads "Urgent Tsunami Earthquake Appeal," and its text exhibits the poor grammar and bad spelling characteristic of many other phishing attempts.

We introduce a novel mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator designed for simulating planetary systems, with a particular ability to accurately handle close encounters. This approach employs a time regularization technique that maintains the stability of symplectic integrators while dynamically reducing the effective time step during planetary encounters. Our method utilizes a high-order MVS scheme, enabling the use of large time steps when planets are distant from each other. This ensures that our algorithm can handle almost direct collisions—defined as situations with mutual separation being a fraction of the physical radius—using time steps similar to those in mildly perturbed systems like the solar system. We illustrate its long-term effectiveness in systems consisting of six super-Earths undergoing significant scattering over 50,000 years. Compared to hybrid methods such as MERCURY, our algorithm achieves superior energy conservation at a comparable computational cost.

In this work, a generalization of the study of human gait was developed based on existing models in the literature, such as those by Keller and Kockshenev. In this hybrid model, a strategy of metabolic energy minimization is combined with a race process using a non-linear description of the movement of the mass center's libration, attempting to reproduce the behavior of the walk-run transition. The results from experimental data for different speed regimes indicate that the perimeter of the trajectory of the mass center is a relevant factor in quantifying this dynamic. An experimental procedure was put into practice in collaboration with the research group in biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratories of Manuela Beltrán University in Bogotá, Colombia.

And after all this, I had to rush to the hospital with my father.

The high-energy tails of diboson production at the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) offer a unique window into the interplay between the Standard Model and potential new physics beyond it, parameterized by higher-dimensional operators. However, the helicity selection rules governing diboson scattering amplitudes pose a significant challenge, as they effectively nullify the leading-order contributions from dimension-six operators, thereby obscuring the interference effects. Fortunately, a precise measurement of the azimuthal decay angles can revive these interference effects, restoring sensitivity to the otherwise elusive operators. A detailed examination of these signatures reveals that the uncertainties associated with higher-dimensional operators in the Effective Field Theory (EFT) framework can be remarkably suppressed through the construction of a bespoke asymmetry variable, which is exclusively generated by the non-interfering operators. This innovative approach effectively demotes the impact of higher-dimensional, interfering operators to the level of statistical fluctuations, thereby enhancing the discriminatory power of this observable.

The nonlinear fluorescence emission has been extensively utilized for high spatial resolution optical imaging. Here, we investigated the anomalous fluorescence saturation effect of nitrogen vacancy defects in diamond. High-power laser excitation led to a notable reduction in fluorescence, which increased the nonlinearity of the fluorescence emission and altered the spatial frequency distribution of the fluorescence image. To extract high spatial frequency information, we employed a differential excitation protocol. By modulating the excitation laser's power, we achieved an approximate 1.6-fold improvement in spatial resolution compared to confocal microscopy. Given the simplicity of the experimental setup and data processing, we anticipate that this method can be applied to enhance the spatial resolution of sensing and biological labeling using defects in solids.

Electric network frequency (ENF) fluctuations offer a powerful tool for multimedia forensics. An innovative method for ENF estimation involves temporal windowing, utilizing the filter-bank Capon spectral estimator. This technique employs a Gohberg-Semencul factorization of the model covariance matrix, leveraging its Toeplitz structure. Notably, it introduces the use of a temporal window—other than the typical rectangular form—before the spectral estimation phase, marking a first in ENF analysis. Krylov matrices facilitate rapid matrix inversions for efficient implementation. When applied with a brief one-second time window in power recordings, this approach surpasses current state-of-the-art methods. In the context of speech recordings, it delivers exceptionally high accuracy and efficiency in terms of both time complexity and result precision.

Shourie raises a legitimate question regarding the credibility of Rajeswar's warnings, given that he, rather than Advani, wrote about the issue in a series of articles published in Hindustan Times. Does the fact that Rajeswar wrote about this issue before it became widely known make his warnings more believable, or does it raise suspicions about his motives and his ability to continue as Governor of Uttar Pradesh?

Spontaneous conversations in natural settings, such as those captured in child-centered recordings, are among the most challenging types of audio to process. However, developing speech processing models capable of handling such diverse conditions would be highly valuable for language acquisition research, where understanding both the quantity and quality of speech children hear and produce is crucial. Such models also hold promise for early diagnosis and assessing remediation outcomes. In this paper, we outline our approach to creating an open-source neural network designed to classify audio segments into four categories: vocalizations by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations by other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To achieve this, we compiled diverse child-centered corpora totaling 260 hours of recordings across 10 languages.

The spreadsheet below provides the implicit commodity based on the effective date of four different rates for the same rate schedule (schedule DR). The rate schedule effective from August 2000 through September 2000 yields a significantly larger commodity charge, as SDG&E customers will receive a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E over those two months. SDG&E customers will expect lower electric bills. Assuming that the lump sum reimbursement is not included in the implicit energy component, the average for June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents and the implicit commodity average from February 2001 through December 2001 is 7.3 cents. The average implicit commodity from June 2000 through December 2001 is 6.9 cents for deliveries starting September 1, 2000, the average would still be 6.9 cents.

Power armor requires little effort; it's practically handed to you early on, and fusion cores are abundant in shops, greatly diminishing its value compared to earlier Fallout games. The story is mediocre, and most aspects of this game are average—except for its stability, which far surpasses Fallout 3's frequent crashes. Is it better than Fallout 3? No. Better than New Vegas? No. This title was massively overhyped for how disappointing it truly is.

A novel, millimeter-scale CMOS-based radiation detector system, comprising a 64x64 array, has been developed for external beam cancer radiotherapy applications. This innovative system leverages a 1x1 μm² diode to quantify the energy deposited by individual charged particles within the depletion region, thereby providing a substantial detection area of 512x512 μm². Notably, the proposed system eschews traditional voltage drop sensing in favor of measuring pulse width, defined as the time required for the voltage to return to its baseline state. This approach effectively eliminates the need for power-intensive and bulky analog-to-digital converters, thereby enhancing overall system efficiency. Fabricated using the TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process, a prototype ASIC has been successfully developed, exhibiting an average static power consumption of 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The system's functionality has been thoroughly validated in a clinical setting, utilizing a 67.5 MeV proton beam, thereby demonstrating its viability for cancer radiotherapy applications.

One single vitamin can significantly reduce the risk of heart disease. Eating chocolate can help reduce stress in students. A new drug has been found to prolong the lives of patients with a rare disease. Health headlines like these are published daily, often making seemingly contradictory claims from each other. There can be a disconnect between the broad, attention-grabbing headlines and the often incremental results of the medical research they cover. To avoid being misled by grabby headlines, it's essential to assess a headline's credibility by looking at the original research it reports on. We've developed a hypothetical research scenario for each of these three headlines. Keep watching for the explanation of the first example; then pause at the headline to answer the question. These are simplified scenarios.

Khyatt (non-privileged).pst El Paso tests market for mainline expansion. El Paso Natural Gas apparently got the message when its last open season for 1.2 Bcf/d of firm transportation capacity attracted bids for 14.4 Bcf/d. The pipeline has announced an open season to test market interest in a mainline expansion and to satisfy regulatory concerns that something needs to be done to relieve the apparent capacity constraint at the California border. El Paso has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand because of enormous demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a Jan. 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to 'assist the difficult situation' now facing the California gas market.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been missing. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity v$_{\rm c}$. We measure v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

Chinese dam builder defies Beijing, continues work on controversial dam despite government order to halt construction. The China Three Gorges Project Corporation is ignoring a government order to stop work on one of its massive dams, according to state-run media. The company is continuing work on the Xiluodu Dam, despite the fact that it is one of 30 large-scale construction projects that have been ordered to cease operations due to a lack of proper environmental assessments. The Beijing News reports that the company may instead choose to pay a fine for its defiance. The firm has also ignored orders to halt construction at two other projects - the Three Gorges Underground Power Plant and the Three Gorges Project Electrical Power Supply Plant. So far, only 22 of the 30 targeted projects have complied with the government's shutdown order.

In 1964, Bob Dylan was 23 years old and at the peak of his career, churning out classic songs at an incredible rate. He was hailed as the voice of a generation, but not everyone was convinced. A small minority of detractors accused Dylan of stealing other people's songs.

In 2004, Brian Burton, also known as Danger Mouse, took things to the next level by combining The Beatles' "White Album" with Jay-Z's "The Black Album" to create "The Grey Album." The resulting mashup was an instant sensation online, but the Beatles' record company was not pleased. They sent out numerous cease-and-desist letters, alleging unfair competition and the dilution of their valuable property. Today, "The Grey Album" is considered a remix.

Two twin domes, two radically opposed design cultures. One is made of thousands of steel parts, the other of a single silk thread. One is synthetic, the other organic. One is imposed on the environment, the other creates it. One is designed for nature, the other is designed by her. Michelangelo said that when he looked at raw marble, he saw a figure struggling to be free. The chisel was Michelangelo's only tool. But living things are not chiseled; they grow. And in our smallest units of life, our cells, we carry all the information required for every other cell to function and replicate. Tools also have consequences. At least since the Industrial Revolution, the world of design has been dominated by the rigors of manufacturing and mass production.

Cabinet ministers have expressed anger over plans to utilize Whitehall funds to keep council tax bills low, a move acknowledged by Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford. In his pre-budget report on Thursday, Gordon Brown reallocated £512m from central to local government budgets. Mr. Raynsford revealed that he had engaged in "pretty frank discussions" with fellow ministers regarding the plans. However, he emphasized that local governments must provide quality services without substantial council tax increases. The central government funding is part of a £1bn package aimed at assisting local authorities in England in limiting next year's council tax rises to below 5%, a crucial consideration in a likely general election year. According to Mr. Raynsford, nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in the effective operation of local authorities.

Robinson aims for dual code success as England rugby union captain Jason Robinson has set his sights on achieving victories in both codes against Australia on Saturday. Robinson, a former rugby league international before switching codes in 2000, will lead England against Australia at Twickenham at 14:30 GMT. Later at 18:15 GMT, Great Britain's rugby league team will face Australia in the Tri-Nations tournament final.  

'Beating the Aussies in both games would be a massive achievement, especially for league,' said Robinson.  

England have the opportunity to secure their third autumn international victory after consecutive wins over Canada and South Africa, as well as avenging June's 51-15 defeat by the Wallabies. Meanwhile, Great Britain could end a 34-year winless streak against Australia with a victory at Elland Road.

We investigate the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero SU(2) triplet scalar, motivated by two-step electroweak symmetry-breaking. We examine two scenarios: one where the triplets are stable and contribute to the dark matter density, and another where they decay through mixing with the Standard Model Higgs boson. The former scenario is constrained by disappearing charged track searches at the LHC and by dark matter direct detection experiments, whereas the latter is constrained by existing multilepton collider searches. Our findings indicate that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is ruled out by direct detection searches, while an unstable triplet with a mass below 230 GeV is excluded at a 95% confidence level.

Interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions presents a compelling approach to unify anomalous Hall and Josephson physics within a single device. We theoretically investigate how the interplay of these phenomena influences the transport properties of superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor junctions. The spin-dependent skew tunneling of Cooper pairs through the spin-active ferromagnetic insulator interface generates substantial transverse Hall supercurrents, which we term anomalous Josephson Hall effect currents. We extend the Furusaki-Tsukada formula—originally developed to quantify conventional (tunneling) Josephson currents—to assess the transverse current components, revealing that their amplitudes can be widely tuned via spin-orbit coupling strengths or the superconducting phase difference across the junction. As a distinct spectroscopic signature of these Josephson junctions, tightly localized subgap bound states emerge near the interface.

While organized crime has a long history, you're right to point out that it's been around for a long time. A fundamental principle in quantum gravity, the weak gravity conjecture, necessitates the presence of superextremal particles in any theoretically consistent framework. Conventionally, these particles are thought to play a crucial role in ensuring that extremal black holes exist in a state of instability or marginal stability. However, the underlying rationale for this requirement remains unclear. This analysis reveals that, within the context of a generic Einsteinian quantum theory of gravity in anti-de Sitter space, the exact stability of extremal black branes is inherently at odds with rigorously established quantum information theorems governing entanglement entropy. Resolving this paradox yields a nonperturbative formulation of the weak gravity conjecture, which converges to the conventional statement under weak coupling conditions. Notably, this argument is universally applicable, independent of supersymmetry or specific ultraviolet completions, and relies solely on the validity of Einsteinian gravity, effective field theory, and holographic principles.

We investigate the connection between two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup $g$ of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface $x_m$ in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial $w_m$. The first, Berglund-H\"ubsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, uses another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface $x_{m^t}$ inside it by some dual group $g^t$. In the second, Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide a simple evidence of the equivalence of these two constructions.

Traditional understanding suggests that an electron primarily exhibits a single form of kinetic energy. However, could it possess additional variations related to its spin, charge, or interactions with other electrons? In one-dimensional (1D) systems, the Tomonaga-Luttinger-Liquid (TLL) model accurately describes the behavior of interacting electrons at low energies, though experimental observations beyond this linear range have been limited. In this study, we present our findings on many-body modes in 1D gated wires through tunneling spectroscopy techniques. We detected two distinct Fermi seas at elevated energies, corresponding to spin and charge excitations, along with the development of three additional 1D 'replica' modes that become more pronounced with shorter wire lengths. The effective interaction strength within the wires was modified by adjusting the degree of 1D inter-subband screening, exceeding a 45% change.

Density-based clustering is grounded in the notion that clusters are inherently linked to distinct characteristics of the underlying probability distribution that governs the data. By acknowledging the existence of a true, albeit unknown, population structure, this approach enables the clustering problem to be framed within a conventional inferential framework. Here, the concept of ideal population clustering is rigorously defined as the partition that arises from the true density function. A nonparametric variant of this methodology, known as modal clustering, establishes a direct correspondence between clusters and the domains of attraction associated with density modes. The operationalization of this approach necessitates the estimation of a nonparametric density function, with the judicious selection of the smoothing parameter being crucial in shaping the density landscape and, by extension, the modal structure that underlies the final clustering partition. This study undertakes an asymptotic examination of density estimation in the context of modal clustering, with a focus on elucidating the theoretical foundations that govern this process.

The study conducted by Nokia reveals that the traditional landline phone in your home may soon become an endangered species. The research indicates that more and more people are using their mobile phone for every call they make or receive. In fact, over 45 million people in the UK, Germany, and South Korea now prefer to use their mobile phone for all their calls. The study highlights that people keep their fixed-line phone for longer calls, but most of those surveyed admitted that the future is definitely mobile. The Nokia-sponsored research found that mobiles and fixed phones are used for different purposes. While home phones are used for longer calls, mobile phones are used for shorter conversations, primarily with friends. In the UK, 69% of those surveyed stated that they switched to their fixed phone because it is still cheaper to use than a mobile.

These vibrations travel to you, hit your eardrums, and then your brain transforms them into thoughts - or at least, I hope that's what's happening. (laughter) Thanks to this ability, we humans can transmit our ideas across vast distances of space and time, sharing knowledge seamlessly from one mind to another.

While I can appreciate the appeal of this game to others, it ultimately failed to resonate with me. At Casa Pescatore, my greatest joy has always been ensuring every guest feels as comfortable and welcome as they would in their own home. Yesterday, I believe I achieved that. Then, in a moment that warmed my heart, my closest friend from Florida walked through the door—a reunion I’d longed for. Ahimsa (uh-him-sah) is a noun that refers to the principle of non-violence towards all living beings. The term is derived from Sanskrit, where 'a-' means 'not' and 'himsa' means 'injury', originating from 'himsati', meaning 'he injures'. As my understanding of ahimsa matured, I became more conscious of my thoughts and speech. Phrases such as "scatter her enemies and make them fall," and "confound their policies, frustrate their knavish tricks" clashed with my beliefs in ahimsa," wrote M.K. Gandhi in his autobiography, "The Story of My Experiments with Truth," published in 1927. Today, we commemorate Gandhi's birthday, October 2, as he was born in 1869. Though he was born over a century and a half ago, Gandhi remains profoundly relevant to our world, with his life and message continuing to resonate with us today.

Paris Promise Lifts Welsh Hopes: Was there ever a more thrilling Six Nations match than Saturday's epic showdown in Paris? And can Wales' resurgence carry them all the way to their first Grand Slam since 1978? These are the questions captivating not only Welsh supporters but rugby enthusiasts worldwide after an electrifying performance in the French capital.  

Welsh icon Mervyn Davies, part of two Grand Slam-winning teams in the 1970s, praised it as "one of the finest displays of the last thirty years." Martyn Williams, who scored two tries for Wales that day, described it as "one of the most surreal matches I’ve ever been part of." Meanwhile, a dejected French coach, Bernard Laporte, remarked simply: "There was a French half—and there was a Welsh half."

More than 2,000 business and political leaders from around the world are converging on the Swiss mountain resort of Davos for the annual World Economic Forum (WEF). For five days, they will engage in discussions on a range of pressing global issues, including China's economic prowess and Iraq's future after this Sunday's elections. Notable attendees include UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and South African President Thabo Mbeki, among more than 20 government leaders and heads of state. In a departure from previous years, the WEF is expected to be less contentious, as anti-globalization campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. Meanwhile, the Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will host the rival World Social Forum, which will run concurrently with the WEF's more exclusive event in Davos.

When I look in the mirror today, I see a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State senator. Manchester's rock band, Doves, have reached a new milestone, claiming the top spot on the UK album charts with their latest release, "Some Cities." A US woman sues over cartridges. A US woman is suing Hewlett Packard (HP), saying its printer ink cartridges are secretly programmed to expire on a certain date. The unnamed woman from Georgia says that a chip inside the cartridge tells the printer that it needs refilling, even when it does not. The lawsuit seeks to represent anyone in the US who has purchased an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, the world's biggest printer firm, declined to comment on the lawsuit. HP ink cartridges use chip technology to sense when they are low on ink and advise the user to make a change. But the suit claims the chips also shut down the cartridges at a predetermined date, regardless of whether they are empty.

Levy wins Whitbread novel prize, orange prize winner Andrea Levy has seen her book, 'Small Island', win the Whitbread novel of the year award. She is now a strong favourite to win the overall prize after beating Booker winner Alan Hollinghurst's 'The Line of Beauty'. Geraldine McCaughrean has picked up the children's fiction award for the third time for 'Not the End of the World'. All category winners go through to compete for the Whitbread Book of the Year title, which carries a £25,000 prize. A panel of judges, including Sir Trevor McDonald, actor Hugh Grant and writer Joanne Harris, will meet up on January 25 to decide the overall winner, with the announcement being made later that evening. Bookmaker William Hill has placed London-based Levy's novel as the 6/4 favourite to win.

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibration of a photonic crystal waveguide (pcw) are reported. The pcw comprises two parallel nanobeams with a 240 nm vacuum gap between them. Models are developed and validated for the transduction of beam motion to phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode (gm) of the pcw for probe frequencies far from and near the dielectric band edge. Since our pcw is designed for near-field atom trapping, this research lays the groundwork for evaluating possible adverse effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near the surfaces of pcws. Longer-term goals are to achieve strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the gms of the pcw, thereby enabling opto-mechanics at the quantum level with atoms, photons, and phonons.

The proposed approach introduces a powerful tool in multimedia forensics, leveraging the frequency fluctuations of an electric network. An efficient method for estimating network frequency fluctuations is introduced, utilizing a novel approach based on temporal windowing and the filter-bank Capon spectral estimator. The proposed method employs a type of Goerg-Semencul factorization of the model covariance matrix, which is feasible due to the Toeplitz structure of the covariance matrix. Furthermore, this approach innovatively employs a non-rectangular temporal window, rather than a traditional rectangular one, prior to spectral estimation. Krylov matrices are utilized for the efficient implementation of matrix inversions. The proposed approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in network frequency estimation, particularly when a short time window of 1 second is employed in power recordings. In speech recordings, the proposed approach yields highly accurate results in terms of both time complexity and accuracy.

We investigate the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero triplet scalar, motivated by two-step electroweak symmetry-breaking. We consider both the possibility that the triplets are stable and contribute to the dark matter density, or that they decay via mixing with the standard model Higgs boson. The former is constrained by disappearing charged track searches at the LHC and by dark matter direct detection experiments, while the latter is constrained by existing multilepton collider searches. We find that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is ruled out by direct detection searches, while an unstable triplet with a mass less than 230 GeV is excluded at 95% confidence level.

Beginning immediately, Enron has acquired control area reserves service from BPA. This enables services to sell unit contingent energy as hourly firm, as BPA will supply reserves for up to 60 minutes. If Grays Harbor Paper notifies RT of a unit derate or outage, RT should immediately notify BPA Dispatch and adjust the schedule accordingly during the next ramp. For instance, if Grays' unit trips at 1333, RT would notify BPA and adjust the schedule to zero for 1400, effective at the top of the hour. The key takeaway is that 60 minutes is the maximum time BPA will maintain the schedule.

We have developed single-shot shaped pulses for ultra-high fidelity population transfer on a three-level quantum system in a lambda configuration. To ensure high fidelity, we utilize the Lewis-Riesenfeld method to derive a family of solutions leading to an exact transfer, where the solutions follow a single dynamical mode of the Lewis-Riesenfeld invariant. Among this family, we identify a tracking solution with a single parameter to control simultaneously the fidelity of the transfer, the population of the excited state, and robustness. We define a measure of the robustness of an ultra-high-fidelity transfer as the minimum percentile deviation on the pulse areas at which the infidelity rises above 10^-4. The robustness of our shaped pulses is found superior to that of Gaussian and adiabatically-optimized pulses for moderate pulse areas.

We report observations of thermally induced transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide (PCW). The PCW comprises two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. We develop and validate models that transduce beam motion into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating through a guided mode (GM) of the PCW, both for probe frequencies far from and near the dielectric band edge. As our PCW was designed for near-field atom trapping, this study establishes a framework for assessing potential adverse effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near PCW surfaces. Our long-term objectives include achieving robust atom-mediated coupling between individual vibrational phonons and single photons propagating in the PCW's GMs, enabling quantum-level optomechanical interactions among atoms, photons, and phonons.

Online reviews provide customers with product evaluations to inform their purchasing decisions. Unfortunately, these evaluations can be compromised by professional spammers who employ sophisticated and ever-evolving spamming tactics to evade detection. The spamming strategies are notoriously difficult to detect, as they can rapidly adapt to new detection methods, target various products, and remain largely unknown in most cases. Furthermore, existing detectors often prioritize detection accuracy over the trustworthiness of product evaluations. To address these challenges, we propose a novel game-theoretic approach where spammers and spam detectors engage in a dynamic competition with each other, driven by practical goals that are not solely focused on detection accuracy. The resulting Nash equilibria of this game lead to robust detectors that are agnostic to any mixed detection strategies, thereby promoting a more trustworthy and resilient review ecosystem.

The global demand for life-saving organ transplants far exceeds the available supply, leaving hundreds of thousands of patients in desperate need of kidneys, hearts, and livers. This critical shortage has spurred groundbreaking innovations in regenerative medicine, particularly in the emerging field of bioprinting. Imagine a future where we could engineer fully functional, personalized organs rather than relying solely on donor transplants. While we haven't yet perfected the creation of complex organs, scientists have already achieved remarkable success in bioprinting fundamental biological structures like blood vessels and tubular networks essential for nutrient delivery and waste removal. This revolutionary technology builds upon the principles of 3D printing, meticulously assembling living tissues layer by layer to construct viable biological structures with unprecedented precision.

At a glance, the UK's opposition has unveiled plans to introduce health checks for immigrants, should they win the general election. Here's a comprehensive guide to the plan  individuals coming to live and work in the UK from outside the EU. If they plan to stay six months or more and are from a country with a high TB risk, they will be required to undergo a chest X-ray and further tests if necessary. All individuals from outside the European Union who wish to stay a year or more will be subject to a full medical examination, covering tuberculosis, hepatitis B, and HIV. A positive TB test result would automatically render visa applications being declined. All other conditions would be assessed on a case-by-case basis.

"The spreadsheet below shows the implicit commodity rate based on the effective date of four different rate schedules from August 2000 to December 2001. The rate schedule in effect from August 2000 to September 2000 results in a larger commodity charge due to a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E to customers during those two months. As a result, SDG&E customers can expect lower electric bills. Assuming the lump sum reimbursement is not included in the implicit energy component, the average implicit commodity rate for June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents and the average implicit commodity rate from February 2001 through December 2001 is 7.3 cents. The average implicit commodity rate for deliveries starting September 1, 2000, is still 6.9 cents.

This game is a damn fine AAA FPS shooter - that's the bottom line. It boasts impressive visuals, exceptional sound design, and outstanding gameplay - with great FPS mechanics, an engaging story, cool characters, flawless voice acting, and a killer soundtrack. However, it's not for the faint of heart, as some cutscenes can be quite brutal and vivid. When a game is this great, its flaws become more noticeable, and here are the ones I encountered: - The lack of SLI support is baffling; I had to put the game on hold until I upgraded my graphics card, as it struggled to run smoothly on my old dual-GPU GTX 590. Make sure you have a decent single-GPU card. - Some ridiculously small obstacles can be frustratingly unclimbable.

I'm recommending this only because it's free and might offer a more enjoyable experience for others. In my experience, however, the game fell flat. Half of its narrative relies heavily on shock value, which neither surprised nor emotionally affected me. This could be due to my own desensitization, or perhaps the content was too simplistic to evoke a response. The other half of the game attempts to portray the mental decline of a character, dealing with themes like obsession, hallucination, and depression, which is intriguing in concept. Yet, the entire experience lasted just 10 minutes, even with my thorough engagement in reading and absorbing every detail.

Though not without its flaws, this game has provided me with immense enjoyment. The unforgiving save system and potential for abrupt character deaths can be frustrating—during my initial playthrough, I lost my primary character roughly halfway through the campaign, desperately wishing for a reload point. This led me to adopt a third-party save tool to prevent such devastating setbacks. Additionally, the undersized aiming reticle and lackluster zoom functionality could use improvement. 

Yet despite these shortcomings, the experience has been exhilarating. As someone who typically avoids zombie-themed media, I've been thoroughly captivated by the brilliant fusion of strategic gameplay, mission variety, and first-person combat mechanics. The game possesses an irresistible "one more mission" quality—whether scavenging for supplies or fighting to reach safety before quitting, I find myself constantly drawn back for just a little more gameplay.

Taking a deep breath in and out... So, music wasn't always my profession. For nearly five years after earning my degree from a prestigious liberal arts college, this was how I made ends meet. *chuckles* I worked as an independent performance artist—the "Eight-Foot Bride," a living statue. I adore sharing this part of my past because people are invariably curious about the real lives behind such unconventional characters.  

Here's how it worked: I'd cover myself in white paint, perch on a pedestal with a hat or collection tin at my feet. When a passerby dropped in a coin, I'd offer them a flower—along with a piercing, meaningful gaze. And if they declined the flower? I'd respond with a silent, wistful gesture of heartache as they moved on. *laughs*

Online reviews play a crucial role in helping customers make informed purchasing decisions. Unfortunately, these evaluations can be compromised by sophisticated spammers who have developed increasingly sophisticated and adaptable strategies to evade detection. The ever-evolving nature of spamming tactics makes it challenging to capture and counter them effectively. Moreover, existing detectors often prioritize detection accuracy over the preservation of the trustworthiness of product evaluations, which can lead to a mismatch between their goals. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach that pits spammers against spam detectors in a game-like environment, where their respective goals are not solely focused on detection accuracy. By achieving a Nash equilibrium in this game, we can develop stable detectors that are resilient to any mixed detection strategies, thereby maintaining the integrity of online reviews and promoting a trustworthy online marketplace.

The sensitivity of the diboson production at the LHC to the interference between the Standard Model and higher-dimensional operators parameterizing the effects of heavy new physics is a crucial aspect of diboson phenomenology. However, the helicity selection rules for diboson scattering amplitudes impose an obstruction to the naive interference contributions of dimension-six operators, causing the total diboson rate correction's leading contribution to cancel. In this scenario, carefully measuring the azimuthal decay angles "resurrects" the interference, recouping sensitivity to the "non-interfering" operators. We delve into these signatures in detail, and find that the uncertainties associated with higher-dimensional operators are uniquely well-suppressed by the construction of an asymmetry variable, which is solely generated by these non-interfering operators, relegating the effects of higher-dimensional, interfering operators to the same status as statistical errors in this observable. Ranking algorithms play a crucial role in online platforms, ranging from search engines to recommender systems. In this paper, we identify a surprising consequence of popularity-based rankings: the fewer the items reporting a given signal, the higher the share of the overall traffic they collectively attract. This 'few-get-richer' effect emerges in settings where there are few distinct classes of items (e.g., left-leaning news sources versus right-leaning news sources), and items are ranked based on their popularity. We demonstrate analytically that the few-get-richer effect emerges when people tend to click on top-ranked items and have heterogeneous preferences for the classes of items. Using simulations, we analyze how the strength of the effect changes with assumptions about the setting and human behavior. We also test our predictions experimentally in an online experiment with human participants. Our findings have important implications for understanding the spread of misinformation.

Carbon is a vital element that is present in all living things, including soil, oceans, and rocks. When plants and other living things die, their stored carbon is released back into the atmosphere, but some of it becomes fossil fuels like coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rocks, while human activities, such as burning fossil fuels, release much more than all the volcanoes on Earth combined. Today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

The palms of your hands are sweaty, your heart is racing, and your stomach is in knots. You can't cry out for help, not only because your throat is too tight to breathe, but also because it would be an embarrassing ordeal. No, you're not being stalked by a monster; you're speaking in public, a fate that some people deem worse than death. When you're dead, you feel nothing; but at a podium, you feel stage fright. However, at some point, we've all had to communicate in front of people, so you have to try and overcome it. To start, understanding what stage fright is, it's essential. As humans, social animals that we are, we're wired to worry about our reputation. Public speaking can threaten it. Before a speech, you fret, 'What if people think I'm awful and I'm an idiot?' It's natural to feel this way, but it's crucial to acknowledge that it's a normal feeling, and with practice, you can learn to overcome it.

This paper presents a millimeter-scale CMOS 64×64 single charged particle radiation detector system for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1×1 μm² diode measures energy deposition by a single charged particle in the depletion region, and the array design provides a large detection area of 512×512 μm². Instead of sensing the voltage drop caused by radiation, the proposed system measures the pulse width, i.e., the time it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline. This eliminates the need for power-hungry and large analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC is fabricated in TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process and consumes an average static power of 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The functionality of the entire system is successfully verified in a clinical 67.5 MeV proton beam setting.

There are currently hundreds of thousands of people on transplant lists, waiting for critical organs like kidneys, hearts, and livers that could save their lives. Unfortunately, there aren’t enough donor organs available to meet that demand. What if, instead of waiting, we could create brand-new, customized organs from scratch? That’s the idea behind bioprinting, a branch of regenerative medicine currently under development. While we're not yet able to print complex organs, simpler tissues, including blood vessels and tubes responsible for nutrient and waste exchange, are already within reach. Bioprinting is a biological cousin of 3D printing, a technique that deposits layers of material on top of each other to construct a three-dimensional object one slice at a time.

Yukos Returns to U.S. Courts: The Russian oil and gas company, Yukos, is scheduled to appear in a U.S. court on Thursday as it continues its struggle for survival. The Russian authorities are dismantling the firm to settle a $27 billion (£14 billion) tax debt. In response, Yukos sought bankruptcy protection in the U.S., hoping to leverage international business law to halt the compulsory sale of its crucial oil production unit, Yuganskneftegas. Despite these efforts, the unit was sold for $9.4 billion to the state-run oil company Rosneft, but not before the state auction was interrupted. Yukos' legal team now argues that the auction breached U.S. bankruptcy laws. The company and its principal shareholders have pledged to pursue any entity that acquires its assets, utilizing every available legal avenue.

The starving orphan pleading for a second portion of gruel. The lonely spinster fading away in her threadbare wedding gown. The cold-hearted miser tormented by the specter of Christmas past. Even a century after his passing, these remain indelible figures from the works of Charles Dickens. So powerful is his literary legacy that it spawned its own descriptive term. Yet what distinguishes Dickens's writing as truly extraordinary? His fiction overflows with suspense, crafted through atmospheric settings, unexpected turns, and enigmatic plots. These signature elements left readers yearning for each new installment. Originally, his tales appeared in serialized form—published chapter by chapter in inexpensive periodicals before being compiled into volumes.

Simon and Garfunkel, industry veterans, placed tenth, earned $24.9 million (approximately £13.4 million) from their comeback tour. Warframe is a fun game for the first few hours of play. After those few enjoyable hours, you'll begin to see that the only thing left the game has to offer is repetition in its combat, enemies, and gameplay. The one major high point for the game is that the developers are working hard and do semi-frequent updates that generally include a lot of content, from sweeping changes to some of the game's systems, new warframes, and other tweaks and features. It's a good game to play casually. Though I doubt it will hold your attention beyond a few months unless you just really like grinding. The upside to all of this is that the game is free, so I'd recommend trying it out. If you're looking for a quick fix, try it out.

Reasoning about graphs that evolve over time is a challenging task in many domains, such as bioinformatics, physics, and social networks. We consider a common scenario where edges can represent short-term interactions (e.g., messaging) or long-term structural connections (e.g., friendship). In practice, long-term edges are often specified by humans, which can be both costly to produce and suboptimal for downstream tasks. To address these challenges, we propose a model based on temporal point processes and variational autoencoders that learns to infer temporal attention between nodes by observing node communication. Since temporal attention drives feature propagation between nodes, using node interaction dynamics to learn this key component offers greater flexibility while avoiding issues associated with human-specified edges.
