When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the conventional approach of using individual survey weights, constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities, to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer yields asymptotically unbiased inference. To address this, we develop a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution, which enables unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. In contrast to frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration, our Bayesian approach uniquely achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Through simulations and a real data example from a survey of business establishments, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across various modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work represents a culmination of recent efforts to integrate traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm, bridging the gap between these two distinct yet rich sub-fields.

Before the world of humans began, there was the world of the gods, comprising fields, plains, and gardens. Four brothers roamed this celestial realm, having no family other than each other - they didn't even know who their parents were. One of the brothers, Deminan, stood out from the others. His skin was covered in painful scars, and he wondered why he alone had been marked with this affliction. One day, while the supreme spirit, Yaya, was out in his gardens, Deminan and his brothers snuck into Yaya's house. After feasting and exploring, they spotted a giant gourd hanging in the corner. But as they tried to look inside the gourd, they dropped it.

New drink limit 'would cut toll': more lives than previously thought could be saved by cutting drink-drive limits by a third, a report says. University College London research claims cutting the limit from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg would save 65 lives a year. The findings are being published by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety as MPs are due to vote on the government's Road Safety Bill. The bill includes changes to speeding fines but not to the drink-drive limit. The research, carried out by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, says cutting the limit would also lead to 230 fewer serious injuries on the UK's roads compared with 2003. He estimates that this would save the country £119m a year. A previous estimate, contained in a 1998 Department of Transport document, suggested such a move could save 50 lives a year.

When random effects are correlated with design variables in sample surveys, conventional methods that use individual survey weights (constructed as inverses of unit inclusion probabilities) to form a pseudo-likelihood fail to yield asymptotically unbiased inference. We propose a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that enables unbiased inference for hyperparameters governing these effects. Our approach is compared with frequentist methods dependent on numerical integration, demonstrating that only the Bayesian method achieves both design-unbiased estimation and consistency with the population-generating distribution. Through simulations and a real-world application involving business establishment surveys, we illustrate our method's effectiveness across diverse modeling frameworks and sampling designs. This research culminates recent advances integrating traditional survey estimation with Bayesian modeling, establishing a meaningful connection between these two rich yet distinct statistical domains.

While electrons are traditionally viewed as possessing a single form of kinetic energy, could their spin, charge, or interactions with neighboring electrons introduce additional energy states? In one-dimensional systems, the Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid (TLL) model effectively describes low-energy electron interactions, though experimental evidence for nonlinear behavior remains scarce. Our study employs tunneling spectroscopy to investigate many-body excitations in gated 1D nanowires. At elevated energies, we identify distinct Fermi seas corresponding to spin and charge excitations, along with three novel 1D "replica" modes whose intensity grows inversely with wire length. By modulating inter-subband screening, we demonstrate tunable interaction strengths with variations exceeding 45%.

The widespread adoption of broadband internet is revolutionizing the way people express themselves online, with a significant proportion of users in the UK leveraging fast web access to share their thoughts, ideas, and creativity with the world. According to a comprehensive report by the UK-based think-tank Demos, nearly a quarter of broadband subscribers in Britain regularly upload content and maintain personal websites, underscoring the profound impact of high-speed internet on online behavior. The always-on, fast connection afforded by broadband is transforming the internet experience, enabling users to engage with online content in new and innovative ways. With over five million households in the UK already connected to broadband and this number growing rapidly, the report's findings offer a fascinating glimpse into the evolving online habits of the British public. Notably, the study reveals that more than half of broadband users begin their day by logging onto the web, while a significant minority even confess to rising in the middle of the night to browse the internet. Perhaps most importantly, the report argues that broadband is empowering individuals to take a more active and participatory role in the online community, fostering a culture of creativity, self-expression, and digital engagement.

As I bid farewell to the carefree days of summer, a mix of emotions swirls within me. My last day of vacation has arrived, and it's tainted by a dentist appointment scheduled for 9 am. Although I dread it, I'd rather confront this obligation now than risk having to navigate it during the chaotic first month of my surgery rotation. The looming prospect of returning to school fills me with trepidation, anxiety, and a deep-seated reluctance. This new chapter promises to be a daunting challenge, one that will push me out of my comfort zone. As someone who strives for perfection in all aspects of life, I thrive on excelling and achieving success. However, the uncertainty and unfamiliarity of new beginnings often prove to be a significant hurdle for me. I've always been someone who resists being confined to a predetermined role, instead yearning to forge my own path and defy expectations. The unknown variables and lack of control that come with starting anew are a source of discomfort, leaving me feeling like a pawn on a chessboard, desperate to break free from the constraints and move forward with purpose.

Chinese Dam Firm 'Defies Beijing': The China Three Gorges Project Corp is refusing to comply with a government order to cease construction of one of its massive dams, according to the Chinese state press. Despite the order, the builder of the Three Gorges Dam continues work on the Xiluodu Dam, the Beijing News reports. The Xiluodu Dam is among 30 major projects halted due to insufficient environmental checks. Instead of halting, the company might opt to pay a fine, according to the Beijing News. The firm has also disregarded orders to stop construction at two other projects: the Three Gorges Underground Power Plant and the Three Gorges Project Electrical Power Supply Plant. So far, only 22 of the 30 construction projects identified by China's State Environmental Protection Agency (SEPA) for not completing mandatory environmental impact assessments have complied with the shutdown order.

Hello. This is my first trip, and the first time in my life I've been outside the walls of Gaza. I'm thrilled to be here. (applause) My lifelong ambition has always been to become a pilot, to fly a plane, to experience the freedom of soaring through the sky, and to touch the clouds. Unfortunately, that dream never took off. Simply put, I live in Gaza, where there is no airport, and all borders are closed on every side. We are essentially living in one of the world's largest prisons. The only thing I can do is gaze up at the sky. On some days, we're fortunate if we have electricity for four or five hours. When it's cold, we resort to making fires on the front porch or on the roof of our homes.

The game offers a compelling RPG leveling system, a wide array of ship types, and physics-based movement for both ships and projectiles. NOTICE NO. 00-338: CFTC APPROVAL OF NEW NYMEX RULE 6.40C

MEMORANDUM

To: All NYMEX and COMEX Members
From: Neal L. Wolkoff, Executive Vice President
Date: October 3, 2000

Subject: Implementation of New Rule 6.40C: Average Price Calculations by Futures Commission Merchants

We are pleased to announce that the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has approved the introduction of new NYMEX Rule 6.40C, which is now in effect. This new rule, attached herewith, aligns with a recent CFTC advisory and provides Futures Commission Merchants (FCMs) with the flexibility to utilize their proprietary systems for generating average price calculations. This applies to instances where multiple prices are received for a single order or a series of orders, subject to specific conditions outlined in the rule.

So, when I was 14, my family was in the process of adopting my little brothers from Ethiopia. And one day my mom asked, "What day should we put for their birthday?" Now, 20 years later, I'm still working on it, except instead of trying to solve the mystery of my brothers' missing birth certificates, I try to solve this problem globally. So, what do birth certificates have to do with international development?

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2d) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2d fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been missing. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2d fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity, v$_{\rm c}$, we measure v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

To do two things at once is to do neither." It's a great critique of multitasking, isn't it? This quote is often attributed to the Roman writer Publilius Syrus, although, as is often the case with such quotes, he probably never said it. What I'm interested in, though, is whether it’s true. I mean, it's obviously true for emailing at the dinner table, texting while driving, or possibly even live tweeting at a TED Talk. But I'd like to argue that for a particular kind of activity, doing two things at once—or three or even four—is exactly what we should be aiming for. Look no further than Albert Einstein. In 1905, he published four remarkable scientific papers.

Spintronic nanodevices exhibit ultrafast nonlinear dynamics and recurrent behaviors on a nanosecond timescale, showing promise for enabling spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. In this study, two physical RC systems were proposed and modeled: one based on a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and another employing 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOs), designed for image classification and nonlinear dynamic system prediction tasks, respectively.  

Our micromagnetic simulations of the nonlinear responses in MSM and STNO devices under current pulse stimulation demonstrate that an RC system utilizing a single MSM achieves exceptional performance in handwritten digit recognition. Furthermore, a physical RC system composed of 24 STNOs proves highly effective in solving complex, unknown nonlinear dynamic problems, as validated through second-order nonlinear dynamic system and NARMA10 tasks.

George Michael to Attend Berlin Film Festival for Documentary Premiere
Michael George, the iconic pop star, will attend the Berlin Film Festival in February to premiere a new documentary about his life and career. The film, which is titled "George Michael: Freedom and Love," will screen in the Panorama section of the festival, which runs from February 10-20. In addition to the documentary's premiere, George Michael will also attend the festival to introduce the screening on February 16.Director Southan Morris and executive producer Andy Stephens will also be in attendance at the festival. The 93-minute film will see George Michael discussing his early days in Wham! as well as his later career, including his legal battles with Sony and his stance against the Iraq war and American politics.

Manchester United narrowed Chelsea's lead in the Premier League to nine points following a determined 2-0 victory over Manchester City. Wayne Rooney capitalized on Gary Neville's cross to the near post by delivering a low shot that deflected off Richard Dunne, giving United the lead. Shortly after, an unfortunate Dunne inadvertently volleyed the ball over goalkeeper David James and into his own net. Steve McManaman squandered City's best opportunity by missing the target from three yards out in the first half. In the initial 45 minutes, United appeared unlikely to secure the crucial win needed to keep their title hopes alive. Their play was more sluggish than strategic, resulting in only one notable chance—a header by Paul Scholes that sailed over the bar.

Sustainability defines the what, where, and how of what is harvested—but the who and why matter most to me. I want to know the individuals behind my meals, understand how my choices affect them, and how their lives intersect with mine. I want to learn why they fish and how they depend on the ocean’s abundance for their livelihood. Grasping these connections transforms seafood from a mere commodity into a chance to heal our ecosystems. It lets us appreciate the food we’re privileged to enjoy. So what do we name this? I believe we call it restorative seafood. While sustainability focuses on endurance and preservation, restoration embodies renewal and advancement.

My journey to become a polar specialist, specializing in the polar regions, began at a tender age of four, when my family relocated from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, Greenland. We settled in the tiny Inuit community of 200 Inuit residents, where we were one of only three non-Inuit families. In this isolated community, we lived without the luxuries of modern technology - no television, no computers, no radio, and no telephone. My days were filled with outdoor activities with the Inuit, playing in the snow and ice, and they became my mentors, teaching me the ways of the polar world. It was there that I developed a profound passion for this unforgiving yet breathtaking environment, and I became truly enamored with the polar realm.

Debra Hill, the screenwriter and producer best known for her role in creating the 1970s horror classic "Halloween," has passed away in Los Angeles at the age of 54 after battling cancer. Hill co-wrote the 1978 film, which featured Jamie Lee Curtis as a babysitter haunted by a psychopath. Under the direction of John Carpenter, the film grossed over $60 million (£31.3 million) globally, setting a record for independent films at the time. Hill also collaborated with Carpenter on "Escape from New York" and "The Fog." Born in New Jersey, she started her career as a production assistant, advancing through the industry to become an assistant director and second-unit director before partnering with Carpenter. Hill is celebrated as a trailblazer for women in film, taking on roles during the 1970s that were typically filled by men.

The Weak Gravity Conjecture (WGC) requires the existence of superextremal particles in any consistent quantum theory of gravity. The standard understanding is that these particles ensure extremal black holes are either unstable or marginally stable, but it remains unclear what goes wrong if this condition fails. This note demonstrates that, for a generic Einsteinian quantum theory of gravity in AdS, exact stability of extremal black branes conflicts with rigorously proven quantum information theorems about entanglement entropy. Avoiding this contradiction leads to a nonperturbative version of the WGC, which reduces to the usual statement at weak coupling. The argument is general and does not rely on supersymmetry or a specific UV completion, assuming only the validity of Einsteinian gravity, effective field theory, and holography.

When a new user signs up on a website, we usually have no information about them, i.e., no interaction with items, no user profile, and no social links with other users. Under such circumstances, we still expect our recommender systems to attract the users at the first time, so that the users decide to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls into the new user cold-start category and is crucial to the development and even survival of a company. Existing works on user cold-start recommendation either require additional user efforts, e.g., setting up an interview process, or make use of side information [10] such as user demographics, locations, social relations, etc. However, users may not be willing to take the interview, and side information on cold-start users is usually not available.

Imagine if you could plug your brain into a machine that would bring you ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If given the choice to sign up for that kind of existence, would you? That’s the question philosopher Robert Nozick posed through a thought experiment he called the Experience Machine. The experiment asks us to consider a world where scientists have developed a machine that simulates real life while guaranteeing only pleasure and never pain. The catch? You must permanently leave reality behind, though you’d hardly notice the difference. Your experiences would be indistinguishable from reality. Life’s natural ups and downs would simply be replaced with an endless series of highs. Sounds great, doesn’t it?

As a child, I was always fascinated by the way things worked and how they could be made. I would spend hours building and tinkering with various projects, from model rockets and slot cars to simple machines and gadgets. One day, my father, who was an engineer, decided to join in on the fun and help me build a go-kart. We spent many weekends together, working on the go-kart, and I learned so much from him about how things were made and how they worked.



But it was the day my father announced that we were going to build a robot that really sticks in my memory. I was about 10 years old at the time, and the prospect of building a real, working robot was both thrilling and intimidating.
At the time, I had no idea how we were going to build a robot, but my father had a plan. He showed me a book on robotics and explained the basics of how robots worked. We would start by building a simple robot arm, and then gradually add more complex parts and features as we went along.
I was excited and nervous at the same time. I had never built anything like this before, and I wasn't sure if I was capable of doing it.

Spintronic nanodevices exhibit ultrafast nonlinear dynamics and recurrence behaviors at a nanosecond scale, promising advancements in spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. In this context, two physical RC systems have been proposed and modeled: one based on a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) for image classification, and another using 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOs) for predicting nonlinear dynamic systems. Our micromagnetic simulations show that the nonlinear responses of MSMs and STNOs, triggered by current pulse stimulation, demonstrate that an RC system incorporating a single MSM excels in recognizing handwritten digits for image classification tasks. Additionally, complex and unknown nonlinear dynamic problems are effectively addressed by a physical RC system comprising 24 STNOs, as evidenced by its performance in second-order nonlinear dynamic system predictions and NARMA10 tasks.

We establish natural splittings for the values of global Mackey functors at orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups. In particular, the restriction homomorphisms between the orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups of adjacent dimensions are naturally split epimorphisms. The interest in the splitting comes from equivariant stable homotopy theory. The equivariant stable homotopy groups of every global spectrum form a global Mackey functor, so the splittings imply that certain long exact homotopy group sequences separate into short exact sequences. For the real and complex global Thom spectra $\mathbf{mo}$ and $\mathbf{mu}$, the splittings imply the regularity of various Euler classes related to the tautological representations of $O(n)$ and $U(n)$.

This space RPG delivers an exhilarating experience with its engaging leveling system, diverse ship selection, and realistic physics-driven movement for both vessels and projectiles. The resource management is intuitive yet rewarding, complemented by a dash of dark humor. While the game avoids excessive grinding when utilizing civilian stations effectively, it does suffer from lackluster AI allies, limited equipment options, and a linear skill progression without branching paths.  

That said, don’t let the drawbacks fool you—this game is an absolute blast. Despite its shortcomings, it offers surprising depth in strategy and variety. The pacing is brisk from the start and only intensifies as you progress. Even with restricted equipment choices, the thrill of unlocking new ships and advancing skills keeps the excitement alive. To truly extend its replay value, mods are essential—*Fight for Universe* stands out as a masterpiece, with *Big Fight for Beef* also delivering a stellar experience.

Ranking algorithms play a crucial role in online platforms, from search engines to recommender systems. In this paper, we uncover an unexpected consequence of popularity-based rankings: the fewer items that report a given signal, the higher the collective traffic they attract. This phenomenon, known as the few-get-richer effect, emerges in settings with few distinct classes of items, such as left-leaning versus right-leaning news sources, and items are ranked based on their popularity. We demonstrate theoretically that the few-get-richer effect arises when people tend to click on top-ranked items and have diverse preferences for the classes of items. Using simulations, we investigate how the strength of the effect varies with assumptions about the setting and human behavior. We also test our predictions experimentally with human participants, shedding light on the spread of misinformation.

Voters flock to blog awards site as voting is underway for the annual Bloggies, which recognize the best web blogs—online spaces where people publish their thoughts—of the year. Nominations were announced on Sunday, but traffic to the official site was so heavy that the website was temporarily closed due to too many visitors. Weblogs have been nominated in 30 categories, from the top regional blog to the best-kept-secret blog. Blogs had a huge year, with a top US dictionary naming "blog" the word of 2004. Technorati, a blog search engine, tracks about six million blogs and says that more than 12,000 are added daily. A blog is created every 5.8 seconds, according to US research think-tank Pew Internet and American Life, but less than 40% of the total are updated at least once every two months.

Even nature's most reviled creatures hold important secrets, but who would want a swarm of cockroaches approaching them? Yet, one of the greatest differences between natural and human technologies lies in robustness. Robust systems remain stable in complex and unfamiliar environments. Notably, cockroaches can self-stabilize while running over rough terrain. When we equipped them with a jet pack or subjected them to a perturbation like an earthquake, we found that their remarkably tuned legs allowed them to self-stabilize without expending any brainpower. They can navigate complex terrain, such as grass, with ease and without becoming destabilized. We also discovered a new behavior, where, due to their shape, they automatically roll onto their side to pass through a simulated grass environment. Robust systems can perform multiple tasks using the same structure, and we have discovered another example of this behavior.

In a remarkable resurgence, Prince has been crowned the highest-earning musician of 2004, surpassing industry icons Madonna and Elton John in Rolling Stone's annual list. According to the US magazine, Prince's lucrative year was fueled by his highly successful Musicology tour and album, generating a staggering $56.5 million in revenue from concerts, album sales, and publishing royalties. This impressive feat relegated Madonna to second place, with earnings of $54.9 million, largely attributed to her global Re-Invention tour. Meanwhile, the legendary duo Simon and Garfunkel made a notable comeback, securing 10th place with a respectable $24.9 million in earnings, thanks in part to their well-received reunion tour. As Rolling Stone aptly noted, Prince's triumphant return to the spotlight marked a significant departure from his decade-long commercial slump. The Musicology tour alone raked in an astonishing $90.3 million in ticket sales, while the album of the same name sold an impressive 1.9 million copies, solidifying Prince's status as a musical force to be reckoned with.

Weight pruning, a widely recognized technique, has been successfully employed as a straightforward and effective method to eliminate redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving accelerated performance on various platforms. However, most pruning methods are essentially trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a novel sparsity dimension, namely pattern-based sparsity, which encompasses pattern and connectivity sparsity, and achieves both high accuracy and hardware friendliness. By designing carefully crafted patterns, our proposed pruning approach consistently and unprecedentedly enhances accuracy and improves feature extraction capabilities on various DNN structures and datasets, and our pattern-aware pruning framework also facilitates pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern, and connectivity pruning, as well as weight training, simultaneously. Our approach, grounded in the new pattern-based sparsity, naturally fits seamlessly into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

Cardinal Criticizes Iraq War: Billions of pounds spent on the conflict in Iraq and the Middle East should have been used to reduce poverty, Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor has said. The head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales made these comments on BBC Radio 4 and will reiterate his stance during his Christmas Midnight Mass. The Cardinal used a Christmas message to denounce the war in Iraq as a "terrible" waste of money. Both he and the Archbishop of Canterbury have spoken out against the war. Speaking on BBC Radio 4's Thought for the Day slot, he criticized the fact that "billions" have been spent on war instead of being used to bring people "out of dire poverty, malnourishment, and disease." The Cardinal said 2005 should be the year for campaigning to "make poverty history.

The notion that attempting to accomplish multiple tasks simultaneously is a recipe for mediocrity is a sentiment often encapsulated by the phrase "to do two things at once is to do neither." This astute observation, frequently attributed to the Roman writer Publilius Syrus, serves as a scathing critique of multitasking. While the authenticity of its origin may be disputed, the phrase's underlying message is undeniably thought-provoking. However, I'd like to propose a counterargument, suggesting that, in certain contexts, engaging in multiple activities concurrently can be a hallmark of exceptional productivity. A case in point is the illustrious career of Albert Einstein, who, in 1905, made a profound impact on the scientific community with the publication of four groundbreaking papers, a testament to the potential benefits of embracing multifaceted endeavors.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the conventional approach of using individual survey weights, constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities, to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer yields asymptotically unbiased inference. We develop a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that achieves unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We contrast our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration, revealing that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real-data example for a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developmental efforts that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm, providing a bridge across the two rich but disparate subfields.

Last night in class, we delved into that week's assigned reading, "Getting Back into Place: Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World" by Edward S. Casey. The first chapter explores "implacement" and the second, "displacement." In the first chapter, Casey distinguishes that our contemporary world and culture are centered around time as the primary situating force, where phrases like "running out of time," "saving time," and "just in time" dominate our discourse. He argues that by placing place at the heart of culture, we can foster a more grounded community. (Please note that this is a rough summary, and I offer this disclaimer in case my professor or classmates happen to read this post.) So far, this book is the first reading that has truly resonated with me.

*(Inhales deeply, exhales slowly)* So, I haven’t always made my living through music. For roughly five years after graduating from a respected liberal arts university, this was my daily routine. *(Chuckles)* I worked as a self-employed living statue—the "Eight-Foot Bride"—and I adore sharing this part of my past because, inevitably, people always wonder: *Who are these peculiar souls in real life?* *(Laughs softly)*  

Here’s how it went: I’d paint myself white, step onto a pedestal, place a hat or a can at my feet, and whenever someone dropped in a coin, I’d offer them a flower—along with a piercing gaze. And if they declined the flower? I’d linger with a wistful, longing gesture as they walked away.

The OSCE, an organization dedicated to promoting security and cooperation in Europe, has been invited by the State Department to monitor the upcoming U.S. presidential election. This marks the first time the OSCE has been invited to observe a U.S. presidential election. In addition, a baker's dozen of Congressional Democrats, including four New Yorkers, have petitioned U.N. Secretary-General Kofi Annan to dispatch international observers to Florida to oversee the election. While I understand the desire for international observation, I humbly disagree with this call. Based on the information available, it appears that the OSCE is coming to learn from the U.S. election process, not to oversee it.

They basically fixed *Shadow Warrior* (2013). The boss fights are awesome, weapons feel very nice (maybe not as good as *D44M*'s, but still solid), and enemies aren't too spongy—unless you're careless with handling elemental and upgrade mechanics. The upgrade system is well-balanced and not too tedious once you get the hang of it. The elemental effects and limited ammo gently encourage you to switch between weapons. There are lots of cool, unique weapons (key word here, unlike *Borderlands*' RNG-based formula) that remain viable for most of the game. The music is great, especially the main theme and, of course, Stan Bush's tracks.

Imagine being able to plug your brain into a machine that would provide you with ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If given the choice, would you opt for such an existence? This is the question philosopher Robert Nozick posed through his thought-provoking experiment, known as the Experience Machine. The experiment invites us to consider a world where scientists have developed a machine capable of simulating real life, guaranteeing experiences of pure pleasure and eliminating pain entirely. The trade-off, however, is that you must permanently relinquish reality, although the simulated experiences would be virtually indistinguishable from the real thing. Life's natural ebbs and flows would be replaced with an endless succession of highs. At first glance, this may seem like an attractive proposition.

If you can overlook the bugs—which, for me, were just two: one minor and one more annoying (both of which have now been fixed, significantly increasing my FPS from 60-90 to 80-120)—I wholeheartedly recommend this as a space exploration game. I noticed that many reviews focus on system specifications, so here are mine: Intel i5 6600k processor (approximately €200), Radeon RX480 graphics card (approximately €200), Z170 Pro-Gaming motherboard (approximately €150), and 16GB of DDR4 RAM (approximately €70), totaling around €650. This set-up is definitely in the mid-range price category, and it’s relatively new. I’m experiencing almost constant 60+ FPS with high settings and V-Sync enabled. I honestly find it hard to believe that 90% of the comments claim to have an overkill setup but still achieve only 20 FPS.

This game... Phew, where to even start? It was absolutely beautiful. From the story to the graphics, I was hooked. The story wasn't just any story; I actually felt like I was that person, trying to figure out what came next in the story my character was telling. The gameplay was great, and I learned a lot about myself through this game (mainly my fear of heights, which I never had a problem with until the ice caverns). My gut churned the whole time, but I persevered for Uncle Fred! If you guys made a DLC or a second game, I would be the first to buy it and recommend it to everyone I know. Tories outlining policing plans: local communities would be asked to go to the polls to elect their own area police commissioner, under plans unveiled by the Conservatives. Party leader Michael Howard said the new role would replace "inconspicuous" police authorities. He said the new office would not supersede the job of a chief constable. The Lib Dems said the plan could let extreme groups run policing, while Labour criticised "extravagant" Tory promises on policing. Responding to the plans, the chairman of the Police Federation of England and Wales, which represents rank and file officers, said it was essential operational independence was retained. Jan Berry said: "It is a service, not a political football to be kicked around every time an election approaches. These plans could result in those with extreme political views dictating what actually happens on the ground," she warned.

This past week has been nothing short of exhausting. I spent Tuesday through Thursday finishing the move to my new classroom. The new room is bigger and closer to my grade-level team—these are the things I need to consider as I spend hours upon hours preparing my room for this year. Had I known I was going to move before the end of the school year, I would have discarded many of the books and supplies I no longer use. Unfortunately, it all came with me. So this week was spent moving, unpacking, and purging profusely. I have to thank my teammate Jill for helping with all of this. You see, she was still teaching this month in my old room, so she and her lovely students moved almost my entire room for me!

For 20 years, I worked in finance. I knew everything about it and was well-versed in the global economy because it was my life. I was familiar with topics such as ecology and the end of the world but didn't really believe in them. Then, in 2007, an extremely unusual event occurred—the subprime crisis and the collapse of major American banks. It was an electric shock. I had several stormy conversations with my wife about this issue, my job, and the limitations of the model. Beyond the crisis, I realized that I was completely wrong and that we were heading for disaster.

Where does all the carbon we release go? Carbon works in a natural cycle, present in all living things: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon makes its way back to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb the carbon. But over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock, and some becomes fossil fuels: coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rock. And human activities, like burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon. Today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

Based on the suggestions from the Monte Carlo experiments in Jiménez (J. of Turbul. 2020), which propose that dipoles are as significant to the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence as individual vortex cores, it has been observed that the kinetic energy of this flow is carried by elongated streams formed through the concatenation of dipoles. Vortices divide into two families: small, fast-moving cores and larger, slowly moving ones, which can be described as 'frozen' within a slowly evolving 'crystal'. The kinematics of both families are distinctly different, with only the former being self-similar. The latter is mainly responsible for the kinetic energy of the flow, with its vortices forming the dipoles and the streams. Mechanisms for the development of this slow component are also discussed.

Let me pose two essential questions that shape my approach to poetry and life. First: Whose legacy carries you? Second: What principles define you? These inquiries form the foundation of every poetry workshop I lead, challenging the misconception that poetry belongs solely to distant figures of the past.  

When I ask students these questions, I also share my personal response—a three-line declaration that grounds me: "I am the child of Black storytellers, born from revolutionaries who shattered bondage and remade history. Their voices summon me. This truth I carry." These words become my ritual, spoken like sacred verse before each performance. Even moments ago, you might have glimpsed me whispering them in the corner, my face alive with their power.

This study delves into the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of Gaussian mixture models, encompassing a variable number of components. Notably, the nonconvex nature of this function leads to the existence of multiple local minima that fail to achieve global optimality, even in scenarios where the mixture components are well-separated. A key finding of this research is that all local minima exhibit a common structural form, which partially reveals the locations of the true mixture components. Specifically, each local minimum involves a distinct combination of two phenomena: multiple Gaussians converging on a single true component, and a single Gaussian encompassing multiple true components. These results hold under a specific separation condition for the true mixture components and remain valid regardless of whether the number of components is over- or under-specified. Furthermore, in the context of three-component Gaussian mixtures, this study yields more refined insights into the interplay between the separation of components and the resulting landscape of the negative log-likelihood function.

Interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions presents an exciting opportunity to integrate anomalous Hall and Josephson phenomena within a single device. We conduct a theoretical analysis to explore how the interplay of these effects influences the transport properties of superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor junctions. The skew tunneling of Cooper pairs, dependent on transverse momentum, across the spin-active ferromagnetic insulator interface, leads to significant transverse Hall supercurrents. We refer to these as anomalous Josephson Hall effect currents. We extend the Furusaki-Tsukada formula—originally established to measure conventional Josephson current flows via tunneling—to assess the transverse current components. Our findings reveal that the amplitudes of these currents can be significantly tuned through the strength of spin-orbit coupling or by altering the superconducting phase difference across the junction. Additionally, as a distinct spectroscopic signature of Josephson junctions, well-defined subgap bound states emerge around the interface.

We introduce a novel mixed variable symplectic (mvs) integrator for planetary systems, capable of fully resolving close encounters. This method employs a time regularisation technique that preserves the stability properties of symplectic integrators while reducing the effective step size during planetary encounters. By utilising a high-order mvs scheme, large time steps can be taken when far from close encounters. We demonstrate that this algorithm can accurately resolve near-collisions (with mutual separations of a fraction of the physical radius) using the same time-step as in weakly perturbed systems, such as the solar system. The long-term behaviour of the algorithm is showcased in a system of six super-Earths undergoing strong scattering over 50 kyr. A comparison with hybrid methods, such as Mercury, reveals that our algorithm achieves significantly better energy conservation at equivalent computational costs.

This is an outstanding strategy game! Having played every other wargame, like European Escalation and ALB, I can confidently say this one is a perfect fit. The variety of units is impressive, and while I can't specify the total number, there are countless options! The graphics are stunning, the sound design is excellent, and the overall gameplay is truly epic. Even the in-game chat box at the main menu provides great entertainment, and I am not exaggerating. However, if you think this game is 'easy' or you're new to the strategy genre, be prepared to invest some time to master it. Don't be discouraged if you lose a lot of units at first. Just remember to plan ahead and always include anti-air and anti-tank measures in your strategy.

When a new user signs up on a website, we typically have limited information about them, i.e., no interaction with items, no user profile, and no social links with other users. In such circumstances, it is crucial for our recommender systems to effectively attract users at the first point of contact, so that the users decide to stay on the website and become active users. This problem falls under the category of new user cold-start, which is a crucial aspect of the development and survival of a company. Existing approaches on user cold-start recommendation often require additional user efforts, such as setting up an interview process or leveraging side information, such as user demographics, locations, social relations, and so on. However, users may not be willing to provide the required information, and side information on cold-start users is often not available, making it a challenging problem to solve.

Prince was crowned 'top music earner' in 2004, earning more than any other pop star, beating artists like Madonna and Elton John in *Rolling Stone* magazine's annual list. The singer banked $56.5m (£30.4m) from concerts, album sales, and publishing royalties with his *Musicology* tour and album. He kept Madonna in second place, as she earned $54.9m (£29.5m) while embarking on her global *Re-Invention* tour. Veterans Simon and Garfunkel ranked 10th, with their comeback tour helping them earn $24.9m (£13.4m) last year.  

"Prince returned to center stage after a decade in the commercial wilderness," the magazine reported. The singer's 2004 tour grossed $90.3m (£48.5m) in ticket sales, and he sold 1.9 million copies of his latest album, *Musicology*.

Online reviews offer product evaluations to help customers make informed decisions. Unfortunately, these evaluations can be manipulated using fake reviews ("spams") by professional spammers who have developed increasingly insidious and powerful spamming strategies to evade existing detectors. These strategies are difficult to capture as they can rapidly change over time, vary among different spammers and target products, and often remain unknown. Additionally, most detectors currently focus on detection accuracy, which does not align well with the goal of preserving the trustworthiness of product evaluations. To address these challenges, we propose a minimax game where spammers and spam detectors compete to achieve practical goals beyond just detection accuracy. The Nash equilibria of this game lead to stable detectors that are agnostic to any mixed detection strategies.

Do I even need to explain why Portal is amazing? Portal is a puzzle game that introduces a concept and then steps aside, allowing you to take the reins. It's an incredible way to challenge your mind while still being entertaining. Although this game isn't as story-driven, it did give birth to one of the most popular games of 2011. I still play this game and beat it when I'm feeling bored, and I love to revisit it to see what spawned a legend and what has become a legend. Gameplay: Portal's gameplay is relatively simple to explain. You utilize portals to your advantage, along with buttons that activate a part of the puzzle. There are two types of buttons in the game: one that you press directly and another that requires a cube to be placed on it.

Sshackle.nsf Notice No. 00-338 October 3, 2000 Memorandum To: All NYMEX and COMEX Members From: Neal L. Wolkoff, Executive Vice President Date: October 3, 2000 Re: Notice of CFTC Approval of New NYMEX Rule 6.40C: ("Average Price Calculations by Futures Commission Merchants") Please be advised that the CFTC has approved new NYMEX Rule 6.40C ("Average Price Calculations by Futures Commission Merchants"), which is attached and is now in effect. New NYMEX Rule 6.40C, which is consistent with a recent CFTC advisory, permits FCMs, under certain conditions specified in the rule, to use their own internal, proprietary systems to generate average price ("AP") calculations when multiple prices are received on an order or series of orders.

02/07/2014 - Unfortunately, I cannot wholeheartedly recommend or fully enjoy this game. In the brief time I've played it, I've realized that I'd be hooked if not for these two critical issues. First, the absence of Australian servers results in a red bar that significantly hampers gameplay and introduces unbearable lag. Even in offline training mode, I experienced stuttering and lag, which is baffling. Combined with the poor ping, it completely detracts from the gaming experience. Secondly, there are rampant graphical artifacts affecting everything from menus and backgrounds to training and in-game content. This issue is unprecedented with my current graphics card, and although I suspected it might be due to my overclocking, resetting the GPU to stock settings had no effect.

Electric network frequency (ENF) fluctuations serve as a potent tool in multimedia forensics. A novel approach to ENF estimation is presented, leveraging temporal windowing based on the filter-bank Capon spectral estimator. By exploiting the Toeplitz structure of the model covariance matrix, a type of Gohberg-Semencul factorization is employed. Notably, this approach pioneers the use of a non-rectangular temporal window in the pre-spectral estimation stage within the ENF field. Krylov matrices facilitate efficient matrix inversions, enabling fast implementation. The proposed method surpasses state-of-the-art ENF estimation techniques when utilizing a 1-second time window in power recordings. Furthermore, it yields highly accurate results in speech recordings, striking a balance between time complexity and accuracy.

Remind me, what did I write about before this? Or, a continuation of the 'is this a date?' saga. After the weird lunch, my confidence shook. I forgot to tell you all an important piece of the story. Before Pat shows up to lunch, Dustin starts talking about what a nice guy Pat is and how he's trying to find a girlfriend for him. I'm listening and nodding, realizing that we're talking more about finding Dustin's best friend a girl than discussing whatever it is you talk about with a stranger over pink lemonade. I excused myself to the bathroom, looked in the mirror, and silently asked myself: Am I being pawned off to the other guy? Is this what happens now?

Wales 'must learn health lessons' - new health minister says. Dr. Brian Gibbons, on his first full day in the job after Jane Hutt was sacked, acknowledged that there are lessons to be learned from England in tackling waiting lists. Despite admitting "big challenges," Dr. Gibbons insisted that the "essentials" are in place. However, both Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats called for policy changes. Meanwhile, Ms. Hutt defended her record, stating that waiting times and lists were "only 10% of the health agenda." Dr. Gibbons, a former GP in Blaengwynfi, in the upper Afan Valley, before becoming AM for Aberavon, emphasized that NHS staff desire a period of consolidation after "tremendous change and reform." One of the significant challenges Ms. Hutt faced during her five-and-a-half years as the Assembly's first Health Minister was the length of waiting lists in Wales.

This game is a disappointing experience, to say the least. I was misled into thinking it was a PC version of 'Katamari Damacy', a game I had fond memories of playing on the PlayStation 2. Unfortunately, it's a far cry from the original. The gameplay is marred by clunky controls and unoriginal level design, which makes for a surreal and unsettling experience. It's not just the gameplay that's the problem, but the game's atmosphere and themes, which left me feeling uneasy and questioning the meaning of existence. Every living creature in this game, from small pets to human beings, seems to be a mere prop, leading me to wonder if the game's creators had a fascination with taxidermy.

Ballymena sprinter Paul Brizzel will be among eight of Ireland's European indoor hopefuls competing in this weekend's AAA's Championships in Sheffield. The only Irish athletes selected so far for the Europeans who will not participate in Sheffield are US-based Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll. Brizzel will defend his 200m title at the British trials, while in-form James McIlroy aims to secure his place in the British team for Madrid by winning the 800m title. McIlroy has been in outstanding form on the European circuit in recent weeks, establishing himself as one of the fastest 800m runners in the world this winter, and appears likely to secure a spot in Madrid. Meanwhile, Corkman Mark Carroll confirmed midweek that he would join Cragg at the European Championships, with Carroll currently ranked third in the world 3000m rankings, behind Cragg who occupies the top spot.

The paper proposes a new approach to temporal graph attention, called Temporal Graph Attention Networks (TGANs), which are based on temporal point processes and variational autoencoders. The key idea is to learn to infer temporal attention between nodes in a graph by observing the dynamics of node interactions. The proposed approach is motivated by the fact that human-specified edges are often suboptimal for downstream tasks, and can be both expensive to produce and difficult to interpret. By learning to infer temporal attention between nodes, TGANs can provide more flexibility while avoiding these issues.
The proposed TGAN model consists of three main components:
1. Temporal Point Processes: TGAN learns to model the dynamics of node interactions by using temporal point processes, which are non-linear functions that take into account the history of node interactions.2. Variational Autoencoders: TGAN uses variational autoencoders to learn the parameters of the temporal point processes. This allows the model to learn a compact and meaningful representation of the temporal dynamics of node interactions.3. Temporal Attention: TGAN uses a temporal attention mechanism to infer the attention between nodes in the graph, which is based on the similarity of their temporal dynamics. Cabinet Anger Over Brown's Cash Reallocation: Ministers are displeased with plans to use Whitehall funds to mitigate council tax increases, as acknowledged by Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford. In his pre-budget report on Thursday, Gordon Brown reallocated £512 million from central to local government budgets. Mr. Raynsford mentioned having had some "pretty frank discussions" with other ministers regarding these plans. However, he emphasized that local governments need to provide effective services without significant council tax increases. The central government funds are part of a £1 billion package aimed at helping local authorities in England keep next year's council tax rises below 5%, which is crucial in a likely general election year. Mr. Raynsford noted that nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in well-managed local authorities.

Low-symmetry 2D materials—including monolayers of ReS$_2$, ReSe$_2$, black phosphorus, group-IV monochalcogenides, and borophene—exhibit far more intricate atomistic structures compared to the simpler honeycomb lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. These materials' reduced symmetries lead to diverse and uneven electron, optical, valley, and spin responses. They also display unique properties such as ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, significant nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Furthermore, low symmetry can result in novel electronic topological traits, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations. The "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" special topic was curated to bring attention to recent experimental and theoretical advancements in the study of these innovative materials.

I watched Troy with my family tonight, but unfortunately, it didn't leave a lasting impression on me. Although I'm too exhausted to delve into the details, I do believe the film had potential. However, I think the filmmakers invested too much in the set design, which was indeed magnificent, and not enough in establishing a cohesive tone and theme. The story was retold, but it lacked the gripping quality that would have made it memorable. I didn't feel like I gained any new insights from watching it, and the character development was somewhat lacking. Furthermore, I was underwhelmed by the score, which seemed to be a less effective imitation of the one from Gladiator. The use of a moaning woman's voice in the background music worked in Gladiator because it complemented a genuinely captivating narrative, which wasn't the case here.

Sir Clive Woodward will coach a Northern Hemisphere team against Rod Macqueen's Southern Hemisphere team in a Tsumani fund-raising match at Twickenham. He said, "I'm looking forward to working with such outstanding players." It will be an opportunity for Woodward to assess some of his options before unveiling his British and Irish Lions touring party, who will visit New Zealand in the summer. "The game promises to be a great spectacle," he said.

Downloads are now being included in the main US singles chart for the first time. Billboard's hot 100 chart now incorporates data from sales of music downloads, previously only assigned to a separate download chart. Green Day's "Boulevard of Broken Dreams" is currently number two in Billboard's pop chart, and tops its digital chart. Download sales are set to be incorporated into the UK singles chart later this year. Digital sales in the US are already used to compile Billboard's hot digital sales chart. They will now be tallied with sales of physical singles and airplay information to make up its new hot 100 chart. The second new chart, the pop 100, also combines airplay, digital and physical sales, but confines its airplay information to US radio stations that play chart music.

Online reviews offer valuable product assessments to guide customer decisions. However, these evaluations can be compromised by fake reviews ("spams") created by professional spammers, who continually refine increasingly subtle and effective spamming tactics to evade detection systems. Capturing spamming strategies is challenging, as they evolve rapidly over time, vary among spammers and target products, and—most critically—often remain undiscovered. Moreover, current detectors primarily emphasize detection accuracy, which does not fully align with preserving the reliability of product evaluations. To tackle these issues, we propose a minimax game framework where spammers and detectors compete based on practical objectives beyond mere detection accuracy. The Nash equilibria of this game yield robust detectors that remain effective against any mixed detection strategies.

I'm recommending this primarily because it's free and might offer a more enjoyable experience for others. Personally, the game left no impact on me. The other half aims to depict (quite literally) a person's mental decline, addressing themes of obsession, hallucination, depression, and more. In concept, it's an intriguing idea. However, it only took me 10 minutes to finish, even as I carefully absorbed every detail.

"In a world where citizens of the globe are facing an unprecedented crisis of monumental proportions, I, António Guterres, Secretary-General of the United Nations, urge you to join me in a collective effort to address the most pressing issue of our time: the climate emergency. We are in a race against the clock, and the clock is ticking. The growing sense of urgency, particularly among the younger generation, is palpable, as the world's inaction becomes increasingly unacceptable. It is imperative that we see a surge in ambition from governments, cities, businesses, investors, and individuals worldwide. I am thrilled to announce the launch of the TED Countdown, a platform that will harness the power of your influence and ideas to accelerate the momentum towards a carbon-neutral world by 2050. This is not only a moral imperative, but a necessity to avert the most devastating consequences of climate change. We possess the tools, the science, and the resources to achieve this goal. Now, let us channel our collective political will

Roxy Music on the Isle of Wight will appear at the Isle of Wight Music Festival, alongside Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. The festival takes place from June 10-12. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzarek, Andy McKay, and Paul Thompson will take to the stage on the Saturday night of the festival. The band is also working on new material, their first since the 1982 album Avalon. Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, McKay, and Brian Eno, with Manzarek joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's 'Virginia Plain', with other hits including 'Street Life', 'Love is the Drug', and the 1981 John Lennon cover, 'Jealous Guy'.

Nestled within the pristine expanse of Canada's boreal forest—the world's largest and most ecologically intact woodland—lies an environmental and industrial project of unprecedented scale and destruction. Spanning the northern reaches of the country, including Labrador, this region shelters the planet's last great migratory caribou population: the George River herd, with nearly 400,000 individuals. Though I failed to spot a single caribou during my visit, their presence is unmistakably marked by the scattered antlers dotting the landscape.  

The boreal forest is a sanctuary of unparalleled biodiversity, particularly its vast network of wetlands—among Earth's most threatened yet vital ecosystems. These natural wonders serve as the planet's lungs and kidneys, purifying air and water while capturing immense quantities of greenhouse gases. Beyond their ecological functions, they sustain an extraordinary array of wildlife, making them indispensable to the health of our planet.

World leaders gather to face uncertainty. More than 2,000 business and political leaders from around the globe are arriving in the Swiss mountain resort Davos for the annual World Economic Forum (WEF). For five days, they will discuss issues ranging from China's economic power to Iraq's future after this Sunday's elections. UK Prime Minister Tony Blair and South African President Thabo Mbeki are among the more than 20 government leaders and heads of state attending the meeting. Unlike previous years, protests against the WEF are expected to be muted. Anti-globalisation campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. The Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will host the rival World Social Forum, timed to run in parallel with the WEF's ritzier event in Davos.

Difference-in-differences analysis can be susceptible to bias when the control group differs significantly from the treated group, particularly due to historical events that impact the groups differently. Reducing this bias can be achieved by constructing a control group that more closely matches the treated group, selecting a subset of the overall control group for comparison. This approach is explored through simulation studies. We specifically examine the impact of mountaintop removal mining (MRM) on mortality using a difference-in-differences methodology, leveraging the increase in MRM activity following the 1990 Clean Air Act amendments. In our analysis of MRM's effect on mortality, we created a more closely matched control group and discovered a 95% confidence interval that includes the possibility of significant adverse effects, no effect, or minor beneficial effects.

Yukos to Pursue Legal Action Against Baikal in U.S. Court Over Asset Sale  

Yukos will appear before a U.S. court this Wednesday to request sanctions against Baikal Finance Group, the obscure entity that acquired its primary asset. The embattled oil company has announced plans to sue Baikal and other parties involved in the $20 billion sale of Yuganskneftegas, seeking substantial damages.  

This legal move follows the Russian government's disregard for a U.S. court injunction issued last week to halt the auction. Yukos' American legal team will now push for a freeze on Baikal's assets.  

The origins and intentions of Baikal remain shrouded in mystery. Russian media reports suggest the firm—which secured Yuganskneftegas for $9.4 billion (261 billion roubles) in a state-orchestrated auction—has close ties to Surgutneftegas, Russia's fourth-largest oil producer. The circumstances surrounding the acquisition continue to raise questions about transparency and legitimacy.

An electron is usually considered to have only one type of kinetic energy, but could it have more, due to its spin and charge, or by exciting other electrons? In one dimension (1D), the physics of interacting electrons is captured well at low energies by the Tomonaga-Luttinger-liquid (TLL) model, yet little has been observed experimentally beyond this linear regime. Here, we report measurements of many-body modes in 1D gated wires using a tunnelling spectroscopy technique. We observe two separate Fermi seas at high energies, associated with spin and charge excitations, together with the emergence of three additional 1D 'replica' modes that strengthen with decreasing wire length. The effective interaction strength in the wires is varied by changing the amount of 1D inter-subband screening by over 45%.

Our research explores the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero SU(2) triplet scalar field, which arises naturally in models featuring a two-stage electroweak symmetry-breaking mechanism. We analyze two distinct scenarios: one where these triplet scalars remain stable and potentially constitute a component of dark matter, and another where they decay through interactions with the Standard Model Higgs boson.  

In the stable triplet scenario, stringent constraints emerge from LHC searches for disappearing charged tracks and from direct dark matter detection experiments. For the decaying triplet case, existing multilepton collider searches impose significant limits. Our findings demonstrate that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is conclusively excluded by direct detection bounds. Furthermore, we establish that an unstable triplet with a mass below 230 GeV is ruled out at 95% confidence.

As I gaze into the mirror today, I behold a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State Senator. I see all of that, and I also see a man who spent a quarter of his life in state prison – six years, to be precise, starting as a teenager on Rikers Island for an act that nearly cost a man his life. However, it wasn't the punishment I faced as a teenager in adult prison or the harsh realities of our legal system that ultimately propelled me forward. Instead, it was an extraordinary learning environment in a classroom that opened my eyes to the possibility of redemption and transformation, something I never thought was feasible for me or our justice system as a whole. A few weeks before my release on parole, a counselor encouraged me to enroll in a new college course being offered within the prison walls, and that decision marked the beginning of a transformative journey that would forever alter the trajectory of my life.

Embark with me on an extraordinary journey—the remarkable odyssey of the Rosetta spacecraft. My profound passion for the past two years has been guiding this pioneering mission to rendezvous with and land a probe upon a comet. To fully appreciate this endeavor, we must first understand our solar system's origins.  

Rewind 4.5 billion years: a vast nebula of gas and dust swirled in space. At its heart, our Sun ignited, while the remaining material coalesced into planets, comets, and asteroids. Scientific theory suggests that once Earth cooled from its fiery birth, comets bombarded our young planet in staggering numbers. These celestial messengers may have been crucial—delivering not only water, but also complex organic compounds. In doing so, they could have provided the very building blocks that sparked the dawn of life itself.

Jamieson issues warning to bigots. Scotland's Justice Minister has warned bigoted soccer fans that she wants to hit them "where it hurts most" by banning them from matches. Cathy Jamieson said exclusion orders are one of a series of measures being considered in the Scottish Executive's campaign against sectarianism. She praised Celtic and Rangers for their work in tackling the problem. However, the minister said stopping sectarian abuse associated with Old Firm matches is a key objective. Ms. Jamieson was speaking ahead of the third-round Scottish Cup clash between the Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The sectarianism long associated with sections of the support from both clubs has become a significant target for the Executive. Last week, Ms. Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell met supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the issue.

"We need to build a weather service for water. However, until we collectively demand accountability, the incentives to fund it will not exist. The first time I spoke at a conference was at TED, eight years ago. Fresh out of grad school, I had no idea that in those few minutes on stage, I was framing the questions I would be asked for the next decade. And, like many 20-somethings, I expected to solve the world's water problems — including the world's water quality problems — with my technology. I had a lot to learn. It was seductive to believe that our biggest water quality problems persist because they are so hard to identify. And I presumed that we just needed simpler, faster, and more affordable sensors. I was wrong.

I am unable to reveal my identity, as doing so would put my life at risk, as the individuals responsible for these illicit activities would likely seek retribution against me. My journey as a journalist began 14 years ago, when I was a young and ambitious reporter, fresh out of college. I had stumbled upon a sensational scoop - the revelation that police officers were accepting bribes from street vendors. As a novice journalist, I recognized the gravity of the situation and the need for a more impactful approach, as everyone was aware of the widespread corruption, yet nothing was being done to address it. I decided to adopt a different strategy, immersing myself in the situation by posing as a vendor myself. Through this undercover reporting, I was able to gather concrete evidence, which had a profound impact. This style of reporting is often referred to as immersion journalism or undercover journalism, a technique that allows for a more in-depth and nuanced understanding of the issue at hand.

Menstruation—a simple word describing a natural biological process—has been burdened by centuries of stigma, reduced to a topic many still discuss only in hushed tones. But why? As I speak to you now, over 800 million women worldwide are menstruating. None of us would exist without it, yet it remains an "awkward" or even "shameful" subject to raise. From my own experience and the stories of women around me, I can tell you it’s exhausting. Exhausting to carefully retrieve a pad hidden in a brown paper bag, tuck it into your pocket mid-lecture, and slip away to the restroom as inconspicuously as possible. Exhausting to sit through classes or meetings, outwardly composed while silently enduring the agony of cramps.

Yes, this is a nostalgia trip for thirty-somethings who remember (at least in the UK) watching the ever-irritating Timmy Mallett just for the five-minute Transformers cartoon. The voice actors for Optimus, Megatron, Bumblebee, and many others are back and instantly recognizable. The distinctive cartoon transformation sound is there. Graphically, the cel-shaded presentation is perfect and very, very Generation One. But aside from that, it's actually a very fun game. The combat mechanics are extremely well done. Transforming mid-fight is often a necessity and woven seamlessly into combos, making it quite unlike High Moon's excellent War for/Fall of Cybertron games. Another distinction is the heavy focus on melee combat, but some enemies (e.g., Seekers) require a switch to a ranged blaster.

While I personally cannot recommend this game, it wasn't absolutely terrible. The gunplay is alright, so long as you have the money to purchase gun parts, which is my main complaint with this game: it's a pay-to-win model. If I could get past that, the game would be enjoyable, as the Hardsuit is fun to use, and HRV is an excellent idea. I'd take this game over any Call of Duty title any day, but its pay-to-win business model is off-putting for me. If you're okay with this model, then by all means, have fun. But if you're like me and dislike pay-to-win games, just let this one slide; it's not my cup of tea.

Estimating the intrinsic dimension of a dataset is a crucial step in many dimensionality reduction techniques. This article introduces "intrinsic," an R package featuring advanced, state-of-the-art likelihood-based estimators for determining a dataset's intrinsic dimension. The package includes methods such as the two-nn, gride, and hidalgo models. To enhance accessibility, it provides user-friendly, high-level functions built on a robust set of efficient, low-level routines. The intrinsic package offers models in two categories: homogeneous and heterogeneous intrinsic dimension estimators. The first category includes the two-nn and gride models, with functions that support inference in both frequentist and Bayesian frameworks. In the second category is the hidalgo model, a Bayesian mixture model, which incorporates an efficient Gibbs sampler for implementation.

After a solid debut on the international circuit, Allan Scott is confident of winning a medal at next week's European Indoor Championships. The 22-year-old Scot finished fourth in the 60m hurdles at the José M. Cagigal Memorial Meeting in Madrid. "It was definitely a learning curve and I certainly haven't ruled out challenging for a medal next week," said the East Kilbride athlete. The race was won by Felipe Vivancos, who equalled the Spanish record. Sweden's Robert Kronberg was second, with Haiti's Dudley Dorival in third. Scott was slightly disappointed with his run in the final, having won his heat in 7.64 seconds but running 0.04 seconds slower than his first IAAF Indoor Grand Prix circuit final.

(With a deep breath, I exhaled) so, I didn't always make a living from music. For about five years after graduating from an esteemed liberal arts university, this was my day job. (laughs) I was a self-employed living statue known as the eight-foot bride, and I loved telling people I did this for a job, because everyone always wants to know, who are these eccentrics in real life. (laughs) Hello. I once painted myself white one day, stood on a box, placed a hat or a can at my feet, and when someone came by and dropped in money, I handed them a flower – and some intense eye contact. And if they didn't take the flower, I threw in a gesture of sadness and longing – as they walked away.

For 20 years, I worked in finance and knew everything about it. I was well-versed in the global economy because it was my life. All the topics related to ecology and the end of the world were familiar to me, but I didn't truly believe in them. In 2007, an extraordinary event occurred—the subprime crisis, the collapse of major American banks. It was a shocking revelation. I had several intense discussions with my wife about these matters, my job, and the limitations of the model. Beyond the crisis, I realized I was completely wrong and that we were heading for disaster.

This study presents a generalization of human gait analysis based on existing models from the literature, such as those by Keller and Kockshenev. The proposed hybrid model integrates a strategy for minimizing metabolic energy with a nonlinear approach to describing the center of mass's movement, aiming to simulate the walk-run transition. Experimental data across various speed regimes suggest that the trajectory's perimeter of the center of mass plays a significant role in quantifying this dynamic. The experimental procedure was conducted in partnership with the research group in biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratories at Manuela Beltrán University in Bogotá, Colombia.

This groundbreaking paper introduces a cutting-edge, millimeter-scale CMOS 64x64 single charged particle radiation detector system designed specifically for external beam cancer radiotherapy applications. A 1x1 μm² diode precisely measures the energy deposition by a single charged particle within the depletion region, while the array's innovative design enables a substantial detection area of 512x512 μm². Contrary to traditional methods that rely on sensing the voltage drop caused by radiation, this novel system leverages the pulse width measurement, namely the time it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline. This approach eliminates the need for power-hungry and large analog-to-digital converters, thereby significantly reducing power consumption. A prototype ASIC was fabricated using the TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process and exhibits an average static power consumption of 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The system's functionality has been successfully validated in a clinical 67.5 MeV proton beam setting, demonstrating its efficacy in real

Ireland surged past Scots, maintaining their Six Nations Grand Slam ambitions with an impressive victory over Scotland at Murrayfield. Hugo Southwell's try gave the Scots an early 8-0 lead, but scores from locks Malcolm O'Kelly and Paul O'Connell put the visitors in command by half-time. A third try from wing Denis Hickie and a third penalty from Ronan O'Gara, who kicked 13 points, extended the lead. Jon Petrie scored a second try for Scotland, but late scores from John Hayes and Gavin Duffy sealed the victory. After two hard-earned away victories, Eddie O'Sullivan's side can now look forward to welcoming England to Lansdowne Road in a fortnight. Scotland will try to give their coach Matt Williams a first Six Nations victory when Italy comes to Edinburgh, but they again struggled to turn pressure into points.

You stand falsely accused of a crime—one you know you didn't commit. The system offers no path to prove your innocence. Maintain your truth and face almost certain conviction and execution, or falsely confess, name others, and walk away. This impossible dilemma confronted hundreds during the infamous Salem witch trials between 1692 and 1693.  

These accusations sprang from a toxic mix of supernatural fear, religious extremism, and a judicial system that prized contrition over justice. Salem, founded in 1626 by Puritan settlers, was a rigid, insular community. Constant conflict with Native American tribes and French colonists further strained an already tense society, creating fertile ground for mass hysteria.

Billboard's Hot 100 chart now incorporates data from sales of music downloads, previously only assigned to a separate download chart. Download sales are due to be incorporated into the UK singles chart later this year. Digital sales in the US are already used to compile Billboard's Hot Digital Sales chart.
